title: PAC Prediction Sets for Meta-Learning

reviews.summary:
This paper builds on PAC prediction sets by extending it to few-shot meta-learning settings, where there is assumed access to a i.i.d. sequence of tasks. In few-shot meta-learning, the goal is to adapt to a new task with only a few training examples. In order to hope to do this successfully, the learner is also exposed to a collection of similar tasks (i.e., i.i.d. from some distribution over tasks) before being presented with the test task. From a confidence point of view, providing theoretically valid, non-vacuous confidence sets for few-shot learning is difficult. The work of [1] first proposed a method of also using the task distribution to help derive meaningful conformal prediction sets, that have marginal coverage guarantees over the draw of calibration tasks and test task. This paper adopts this setting, but casts it under the framework of PAC prediction sets, which offer high probability guarantees about the expected mis-coverage over new tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks. On a high-level, PAC sets is to CP sets as Meta-PAC sets are to Meta-CP sets, and offer a different set of (desireable) performance guarantees. The paper empirically validates the effectiveness (and theoretical properties) of their algorithm on simulated few-shot meta-learning datasets (i.e., K-shot N-way image classification and relation classification tasks), as well as a realistic heart attack prediction task (where the "tasks" are stratified by year and state).

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper is well-written (for the most part, see below) and the experiments are interesting. As the authors do well in pointing out, their PAC calibration framework offers several desireable guarantees that are not covered by the Meta-CP procedure. For example, Meta-PAC sets will be valid on average across all new tasks, rather than just on the (n + 1)th, marginalized over the draw of tasks {1, ..., n + 1}. Like standard PAC sets, this is nice, as it is often more practical to assume that the same fixed calibration set is reused for predictions across many future tasks. Therefore, even though prediction sets have been considered in a meta-learning setting before, this seems like a meaningful contribution.

Apart from a few specific comments/concerns, my main remark is on the readability of the presentation. Granted, I think the paper does an ok job considering the number of factors to consider simultaneously, but the notation is quite burdensome. It may be much easier to follow if a high-level, step-by-step description of what the authors want to accomplish and how they do it, is provided at the start of section 4---instead of jumping right into notation. For example, at a high-level, Meta-PAC sets are trying to find a single, shared threshold to use that 
1. On average over the random draw of calibration tasks that are used to choose this threshold...
2. and on average over the random choice of task that is used to evaluate this threshold...
3. and on average over predictions the model makes using this threshold on the new task...
4. The prediction set contains the correct label.

(Where we can replace "average" with high probability $1-\delta$, $1-\alpha$, $1-\epsilon$.)

I think this message is hinted at in various places in the manuscript, but it would be useful to say it plainly up front (obviously, not exactly in those words, but in simple, intuitive terms).

On to a few high-lighted strengths...

- The theoretical setting is appealing, and a reasonable and useful extension to Meta-CP (as stated above). 
- The calibration strategy seems correct and nice. At first I was concerned that computing PAC-thresholds for each of the calibration tasks was missing a factor of 1/N so that they are valid simultaneously, before applying the PAC method again on top of these values---as, with high probability, many $\hat{\tau}_{\epsilon, \alpha/2}(S_i, A_i)$ will _not_ be in $T_\epsilon(\theta_i, A_i)$ for large N. So we'd be calibrating the final threshold based on some "imposter" thresholds from a subset of tasks {1, ..., N}. However, my understanding is that since we only care about the output of a PAC method being i.i.d. (this is the r.v. O = PS-Binom(...) where O is valid w.p. 1 - alpha), not the output being valid itself, we do not actually need the PAC property to hold simulataneously over calibration tasks. Hence, the authors are able to avoid the 1/N factor in favor of two 1/2 factors (which is way more efficient). 
- Empirical results seem strong.

And a few weaknesses...

- It's kind of bothersome that a single threshold is used for _all_ downstream tasks; i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to also estimate a data-dependent threshold (i.e., the adjusted quantile prediction). Therefore, when there is enough few-shot data, or significant variability across tasks, this could be better (in terms of being test-task conditionally). Here, the threshold never changes, regardless of whether the new task seems incredibly hard or incredibly easy.
- How tight are Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes? Assuming access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks seems rather unrealistic, apart from very specific scenarios. The theory is technically solid, but a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration). Thus I anticipate it to have only a relatively modest impact.
reviews.questions:
- In the experiments it seems that you calibrate using tasks sampled from N choose k base calbration classes, and test on N choose k base (disjoint) test classes. Samples from the second are not i.i.d. w.r.t the second?
 - Lines 171-174 I can't understand. From what I can see, you simply want to estimate a value t s.t. $P(\hat{\tau}_{\alpha/2, \delta}(S) \leq t) \geq 1 - \delta$. I'm not sure what $g$ is, and adding the dummy label also seems confusing.
reviews.limitations: The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. It might be worth to expand on the last sentence of the conclusion: namely that people should understand that this algorithm is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation, but one that relies on several strong assumptions, and only holds on average across new tasks. That said, it is better than nothing!
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

In few-shot meta-learning, the goal is to adapt to a new task with only a few training examples. In order to hope to do this successfully, the learner is also exposed to a collection of similar tasks (i.e., i.i.d. from some distribution over tasks) before being presented with the test task. From a confidence point of view, providing theoretically valid, non-vacuous confidence sets for few-shot learning is difficult. The work of [1] first proposed a method of also using the task distribution to help derive meaningful conformal prediction sets, that have marginal coverage guarantees over the draw of calibration tasks and test task. This paper adopts this setting, but casts it under the framework of PAC prediction sets, which offer high probability guarantees about the expected mis-coverage over new tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks. On a high-level, PAC sets is to CP sets as Meta-PAC sets are to Meta-CP sets, and offer a different set of (desireable) performance guarantees. The paper empirically validates the effectiveness (and theoretical properties) of their algorithm on simulated few-shot meta-learning datasets (i.e., K-shot N-way image classification and relation classification tasks), as well as a realistic heart attack prediction task (where the "tasks" are stratified by year and state).

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
The paper is well-written (for the most part, see below) and the experiments are interesting. As the authors do well in pointing out, their PAC calibration framework offers several desireable guarantees that are not covered by the Meta-CP procedure. For example, Meta-PAC sets will be valid on average across all new tasks, rather than just on the (n + 1)th, marginalized over the draw of tasks {1, ..., n + 1}. Like standard PAC sets, this is nice, as it is often more practical to assume that the same fixed calibration set is reused for predictions across many future tasks. Therefore, even though prediction sets have been considered in a meta-learning setting before, this seems like a meaningful contribution.

Apart from a few specific comments/concerns, my main remark is on the readability of the presentation. Granted, I think the paper does an ok job considering the number of factors to consider simultaneously, but the notation is quite burdensome. It may be much easier to follow if a high-level, step-by-step description of what the authors want to accomplish and how they do it, is provided at the start of section 4---instead of jumping right into notation. For example, at a high-level, Meta-PAC sets are trying to find a single, shared threshold to use that 
1. On average over the random draw of calibration tasks that are used to choose this threshold...
2. and on average over the random choice of task that is used to evaluate this threshold...
3. and on average over predictions the model makes using this threshold on the new task...
4. The prediction set contains the correct label.

(Where we can replace "average" with high probability $1-\delta$, $1-\alpha$, $1-\epsilon$.)

I think this message is hinted at in various places in the manuscript, but it would be useful to say it plainly up front (obviously, not exactly in those words, but in simple, intuitive terms).

On to a few high-lighted strengths...

- The theoretical setting is appealing, and a reasonable and useful extension to Meta-CP (as stated above). 
- The calibration strategy seems correct and nice. At first I was concerned that computing PAC-thresholds for each of the calibration tasks was missing a factor of 1/N so that they are valid simultaneously, before applying the PAC method again on top of these values---as, with high probability, many $\hat{\tau}_{\epsilon, \alpha/2}(S_i, A_i)$ will _not_ be in $T_\epsilon(\theta_i, A_i)$ for large N. So we'd be calibrating the final threshold based on some "imposter" thresholds from a subset of tasks {1, ..., N}. However, my understanding is that since we only care about the output of a PAC method being i.i.d. (this is the r.v. O = PS-Binom(...) where O is valid w.p. 1 - alpha), not the output being valid itself, we do not actually need the PAC property to hold simulataneously over calibration tasks. Hence, the authors are able to avoid the 1/N factor in favor of two 1/2 factors (which is way more efficient). 
- Empirical results seem strong.

And a few weaknesses...

- It's kind of bothersome that a single threshold is used for _all_ downstream tasks; i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to also estimate a data-dependent threshold (i.e., the adjusted quantile prediction). Therefore, when there is enough few-shot data, or significant variability across tasks, this could be better (in terms of being test-task conditionally). Here, the threshold never changes, regardless of whether the new task seems incredibly hard or incredibly easy.
- How tight are Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes? Assuming access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks seems rather unrealistic, apart from very specific scenarios. The theory is technically solid, but a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration). Thus I anticipate it to have only a relatively modest impact.

Questions:
- In the experiments it seems that you calibrate using tasks sampled from N choose k base calbration classes, and test on N choose k base (disjoint) test classes. Samples from the second are not i.i.d. w.r.t the second?

The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. It might be worth to expand on the last sentence of the conclusion: namely that people should understand that this algorithm is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation, but one that relies on several strong assumptions, and only holds on average across new tasks. That said, it is better than nothing!

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Summary

This work extends PAC prediction sets to the meta-learning paradigm, where the learner has access to a distribution of related tasks. The authors propose a framework for constructing prediction sets in few-shot learning scenarios that leverages task-level calibration data. Building on prior work in meta-conformal prediction (specifically [1]), they reformulate the problem through a PAC-learning lens to obtain high-probability guarantees on average mis-coverage across new tasks, conditioned on a fixed calibration set. The key distinction from existing meta-conformal approaches is that their method provides task-agnostic coverage guarantees suitable for reusing a single calibration set across many downstream tasks. The framework is evaluated on standard few-shot benchmarks (K-shot N-way classification, relation classification) and a practical application involving heart attack risk prediction stratified by temporal and geographic factors.

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.

## Strengths

- **Well-motivated theoretical contribution**: The PAC prediction set framework offers distinct advantages over existing meta-conformal approaches. Specifically, Meta-PAC sets provide validity guarantees that hold in expectation across all future tasks rather than only marginalizing over the joint draw of calibration and test tasks. This practical distinction is valuable when a single calibration set must support predictions on numerous downstream tasks.

- **Sound calibration methodology**: The two-stage calibration procedure is technically correct and elegant. I initially worried whether computing PAC-thresholds for individual calibration tasks required a union bound correction (a 1/N factor) to ensure simultaneous validity before applying the outer PAC method. Upon reflection, however, the key insight is that the PAC method only requires the intermediate outputs to be i.i.d. (specifically, O ~ PS-Binom(...)), not individually valid. This allows the authors to avoid the 1/N penalty in favor of two 1/2 factors, yielding substantially better sample efficiency.

- **Solid empirical validation**: The experimental results demonstrate the practical viability of the proposed approach across multiple domains.

- **Clear exposition (mostly)**: The manuscript is generally well-presented, making a reasonable effort to manage the notational complexity inherent in the problem.

## Weaknesses

- **Presentation could be more intuitive**: While the paper manages the notation reasonably well, the readability would benefit significantly from a high-level conceptual walkthrough before diving into formal definitions. Specifically, Section 4 would be strengthened by an explicit, plain-language explanation of the core objective. For instance: Meta-PAC sets aim to find a single shared threshold such that (1) on average over the random draw of calibration tasks used to select this threshold, (2) on average over the random selection of the test task, and (3) on average over predictions made using this threshold, the prediction set contains the correct label with high probability 1-δ, 1-α, 1-ε. This intuition is scattered throughout the manuscript but would be more impactful stated upfront in accessible terms.

- **Fixed threshold across tasks may be suboptimal**: A notable limitation is that the learned threshold τ remains constant across all downstream tasks and does not adapt based on the size or characteristics of the adaptation set A. In contrast, Meta-CP methods attempt to estimate task-dependent thresholds (e.g., adjusted quantiles) that can respond to the difficulty or variability of individual tasks. This means that when abundant few-shot data is available or when task difficulty varies substantially, the Meta-PAC approach may produce unnecessarily conservative or loose prediction sets compared to adaptive alternatives.

- **Scalability concerns with calibration data**: The reliance on Clopper-Pearson bounds becomes problematic when calibration task sets are small. The requirement for several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks is demanding and unrealistic in many practical scenarios beyond specialized domains. While the underlying theory is sound, the approach represents a relatively straightforward combination of existing techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration), suggesting limited novelty and potentially modest real-world impact given these practical constraints.

## Questions and Clarifications

- **Experiment design**: In the experiments, calibration tasks are sampled from N choose k base classes, while test tasks are drawn from disjoint N choose k base classes. Are samples from the test set truly i.i.d. with respect to the task distribution, or does the class-disjointness constraint introduce dependencies?

- **Technical clarity (Lines 171-174)**: The description of the threshold estimation procedure is unclear. The stated objective appears to be finding t such that P(τ̂_{α/2, δ}(S) ≤ t) ≥ 1 - δ, but the role of g and the purpose of the dummy label are not well-explained.

## Limitations and Broader Impact

The authors adequately discuss limitations and societal impact. However, the conclusion would benefit from expanded discussion of the final point: namely, that this method is not a universal solution for few-shot confidence estimation but relies on strong assumptions (particularly the i.i.d. task assumption) and provides only average-case guarantees across new tasks. While these limitations are important to acknowledge, they do not negate the value of having a theoretically grounded approach, even if imperfect.

## Overall Assessment

This paper makes a technically sound contribution by extending PAC prediction sets to meta-learning settings. The theoretical framework is correct, and the practical advantages over existing meta-conformal methods are genuine. The experimental validation is competent, and the work addresses a meaningful problem. The primary areas for improvement are presentational clarity and acknowledgment of practical constraints on calibration data requirements. The contribution is solid but somewhat incremental, combining established techniques in a natural way without substantial algorithmic or theoretical innovation. The work merits acceptance as a technically correct paper with moderate practical value, though it is unlikely to generate significant downstream impact given its reliance on assumptions that limit applicability.

**Rating: 6 - Weak Accept**: The paper is technically sound with no major concerns regarding evaluation, resources, or reproducibility. It represents a meaningful but incremental advance with moderate practical utility.

reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:


* The paper is well-written.
* The proposed method is reasonable with theoretical analysis.
* Experiments on various domain and datasets are compared.


Weakness:



* For reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, would be better if the meta predictions set parameters are also adapted?
* The current methods seem only applies on metric-based meta-learning methods, e.g., prototypical network. It would be better if the methods could be also applies on optimization-based methods, such as model-agnostic meta learning (MAML).
* For mini-ImageNet datasets, only 5-way classification problem is compared. It would be better if the authors can compare on more challenging, 10-way or 20-way classification.

Summary:
This paper proposes a PAC prediction set algorithm for meta learning. The goal is to adapt the predictor to new unseen tasks with a few labeled training examples. Experiments on four datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods with  PAC guarantee while having smaller prediction set size.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:


* The paper is well-written.
* The proposed method is reasonable with theoretical analysis.
* Experiments on various domain and datasets are compared.


Weakness:



* For reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, would be better if the meta predictions set parameters are also adapted?
* The current methods seem only applies on metric-based meta-learning methods, e.g., prototypical network. It would be better if the methods could be also applies on optimization-based methods, such as model-agnostic meta learning (MAML).
* For mini-ImageNet datasets, only 5-way classification problem is compared. It would be better if the authors can compare on more challenging, 10-way or 20-way classification.

Questions:
Please refer to weaknesss above.

Limitations:
Yes, the authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Peer Review (Rewritten)

## Summary

The paper introduces a PAC prediction set framework designed for meta-learning scenarios, where the objective is to quickly calibrate predictors to novel tasks using limited labeled examples. The empirical evaluation across four different datasets shows that the proposed approach achieves PAC guarantees while simultaneously reducing the size of prediction sets compared to existing methods.

## Strengths

* The manuscript demonstrates clear and coherent presentation throughout.
* The proposed methodology is grounded in solid theoretical foundations with accompanying formal analysis.
* The experimental evaluation encompasses comparisons across multiple datasets and application domains.

## Weaknesses

* A significant limitation concerns the reuse of meta-learned prediction set parameters without subsequent task-specific adaptation. It would strengthen the work if these parameters were themselves refined during the adaptation phase to new tasks.
* The applicability of the current approach appears restricted to metric-learning based meta-learning frameworks, such as prototypical networks. Extension to optimization-based paradigms, particularly model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML), would substantially broaden the method's scope and relevance.
* The mini-ImageNet experiments are limited to 5-way classification settings. The evaluation would be considerably more convincing if the authors demonstrated performance on more challenging scenarios, such as 10-way or 20-way classification problems.

## Questions

Please see the weaknesses section above for detailed concerns requiring clarification and further investigation.

## Limitations

The authors have appropriately acknowledged the limitations of their work and have given due consideration to potential societal implications.

## Recommendation

**6: Weak Accept** — The paper presents technically sound contributions with moderate-to-high potential impact. No significant issues are identified regarding experimental rigor, resource requirements, reproducibility, or ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of prediction set, which uses a two-step strategy to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold on each of N distributions, and then use these thresholds (with a label 1 attached to each threshold) to construct a new sample of size N to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Originality:
This paper generalizes PAC notation of conformal prediction from previous studies to mPAC in meta learning case, where multiple distributions are present, augmented calibration set is available. Algorithm is proposed to guarantee mPAC built on the existing algorithm designed for PAC prediction. 

Quality:
This paper is technically sound and theoretical analysis is clear.

Clarity:
This paper is easy to follow.

Significance: 
This paper provides insights to conformal prediction in meta learning setting.
reviews.questions: Algorithm 2 involves grid search for $\tau$. Could the authors elaborate a bit more on its computational cost/efficiency in analysis or experiment (e.g., the Granularity for searching)? Would there be any possible way to accelerate such grid search?

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of prediction set, which uses a two-step strategy to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold on each of N distributions, and then use these thresholds (with a label 1 attached to each threshold) to construct a new sample of size N to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Originality:
This paper generalizes PAC notation of conformal prediction from previous studies to mPAC in meta learning case, where multiple distributions are present, augmented calibration set is available. Algorithm is proposed to guarantee mPAC built on the existing algorithm designed for PAC prediction. 

Quality:
This paper is technically sound and theoretical analysis is clear.

Clarity:
This paper is easy to follow.

Significance: 
This paper provides insights to conformal prediction in meta learning setting.

Questions:
Algorithm 2 involves grid search for $\tau$. Could the authors elaborate a bit more on its computational cost/efficiency in analysis or experiment (e.g., the Granularity for searching)? Would there be any possible way to accelerate such grid search?

Limitations:
I think the authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Summary

The paper addresses the problem of constructing prediction sets within a meta-learning framework while maintaining PAC-style theoretical guarantees. The authors recognize that the presence of multiple task distributions in meta-learning necessitates a departure from classical PAC notions used in standard machine learning and from existing approaches for handling distribution shift (such as importance weighting methods). The key contribution is the introduction of meta-PAC (mPAC)—a formal framework (Definition 3) that leverages calibration data from N source distributions to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction sets for an unseen target distribution with high probability $1-\delta$. The proposed Algorithm 1 employs a hierarchical two-stage approach: first, it derives ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC thresholds across each of the N training distributions independently, then combines these thresholds (augmented with positive labels) into a secondary dataset of size N to establish a final threshold satisfying ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC guarantees. Theorem 1 provides theoretical justification for this algorithmic strategy under the mPAC framework.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

**Originality:**
The work makes a meaningful contribution by extending the conformal prediction framework beyond its traditional scope to accommodate the multi-distribution setting inherent to meta-learning. The adaptation of PAC guarantees to this context, combined with the availability of task-specific calibration data, represents a natural yet non-trivial generalization of prior work.

**Quality:**
The theoretical development is sound and the mathematical exposition is rigorous. The analysis supporting the main results is presented clearly and appears to be free of significant technical errors.

**Clarity:**
The paper is well-written and accessible. The motivation for the proposed framework is clearly articulated, and the progression from problem formulation to algorithmic solution is logical and easy to follow.

**Significance:**
The work offers valuable theoretical insights into applying conformal prediction methods within the meta-learning paradigm, contributing to a better understanding of prediction guarantees in this increasingly important setting.

## Questions and Suggestions

A notable point concerns Algorithm 2, which relies on grid search to identify the optimal value of $\tau$. The authors would benefit from providing more detailed discussion regarding the computational complexity and practical efficiency of this approach. Specifically, clarification on the grid granularity employed and discussion of potential strategies for accelerating the search procedure—whether through adaptive refinement, binary search variants, or other optimization techniques—would strengthen the practical applicability of the method.

## Limitations

The authors have appropriately acknowledged the limitations of their work and have given adequate consideration to potential societal impacts, demonstrating responsible scholarship.

## Overall Assessment

This paper presents technically sound work with moderate-to-high potential impact. The theoretical contributions are solid, the evaluation is appropriately conducted, and there are no major concerns regarding reproducibility, computational resources, or ethical considerations. While not groundbreaking, the work makes a useful and timely contribution to the intersection of conformal prediction and meta-learning.

**Recommendation: Accept (Weak Accept)**

reviews.summary:
POST DISCUSSION:

The authors addressed most of my issues about readability and the attribution problem, so I have increased my score to a weak accept.

My final assessment is as follows:
I believe the proofs are correct, and the related work is correct.
I think the paper is the first to do a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which has some value. Although this guarantee is a straightforward extension of existing results, perhaps somebody might be interested in it, and I do not believe it has appeared before.

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. in that it also conditions on the tasks. Maybe somebody will find that useful!

_____________
This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.
reviews.questions: What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?
reviews.limitations: I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)

My final assessment is as follows:

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. in that it also conditions on the tasks. Maybe somebody will find that useful!

_____________
This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.

Questions:
What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?

Limitations:
I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

Following the authors' revisions that substantially improved the paper's presentation and resolved the attribution concerns I initially raised, I have upgraded my recommendation to a weak accept.

My overall assessment is as follows:
The mathematical proofs appear sound, and the related work section accurately represents the current state of the field. To my knowledge, this represents the first work to establish a two-stage training-conditional guarantee in this context. While the theoretical contribution builds incrementally on existing techniques, this particular combination appears novel and could be of interest to the community. Additionally, this work constitutes the second application of training-conditional conformal methods to meta-learning, and it distinguishes itself from prior work by Adam Fisch and colleagues through its task-conditioning approach, which may prove valuable for future applications.

## Technical Content and Contribution

The paper develops a distribution-free uncertainty quantification framework for meta-learning with a triply-nested guarantee structure. The innermost component involves an expectation over a binary loss function, which is then nested within two additional probability bounds that condition on both the calibration data and the learned meta-parameters. The proposed algorithmic approach elegantly applies binomial concentration bounds twice in succession. The experimental evaluation is thorough, spanning diverse domains including computer vision, natural language processing, and medical applications.

## Strengths

- The theoretical results are mathematically sound and rigorously derived
- The experimental evaluation is comprehensive and covers multiple application domains

## Weaknesses

(1) The guarantee itself is challenging to interpret in practice. The nested structure with multiple conditioning variables and parameters creates substantial cognitive burden for practitioners attempting to apply the method. Adding these additional layers of conditioning to an already complex tolerance interval framework raises questions about practical usability and parameter selection in real-world scenarios.

(2) The presentation quality significantly hampers accessibility. The writing is dense and technically intricate in ways that obscure rather than clarify. Consider Figure 2 as an example: it contains superfluous details, lacks color coding, and leaves key visual elements unexplained—what do the stripe orientations represent, and what does the box labeled "N" signify? Similarly, Section 4.1 attempts to pack too much content into limited space, making it difficult for readers outside the narrow subfield of PAC-style guarantees to follow the exposition.

(3) The theoretical novelty is somewhat limited, and the problem setting itself is not particularly unexplored. The existing meta-conformal prediction literature already provides workable guarantees for this domain, though admittedly marginal with respect to the meta-learned parameters. The core technical contribution—applying binomial concentration twice instead of once to enable additional conditioning—represents a relatively straightforward extension of established results. That said, the clever chaining of two concentration arguments to achieve this goal is commendable.

(4) The paper contains significant attribution errors that require correction. The concept of training-conditional prediction sets originates from Vovk [21] and should be exclusively credited to that work. The current practice of referring to these as "PAC prediction sets [3]" and dual-citing both sources misrepresents the intellectual history. While paper [3] merits credit for extending Vovk's ideas to deep learning contexts, [21] must be recognized as the sole originator of this foundational concept. The same principle applies to the PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially equivalent to Vovk's Proposition 2b. Furthermore, the rebranding from "training-conditional validity" to "PAC prediction sets" obscures the fact that these are equivalent concepts, with the former terminology having established priority.

## Questions for Authors

In practical, non-synthetic applications, what does the assumption that tasks are drawn identically and independently actually entail? Can you provide concrete, realistic scenarios where the proposed guarantee would reasonably be expected to hold?

## Limitations

The authors have adequately addressed the previously identified limitations. I find no serious ethical concerns with this work, and ethical review should not impede its publication.

## Recommendation

**6: Weak Accept** - The paper presents technically sound results with moderate-to-high impact potential. No major concerns exist regarding experimental rigor, resource requirements, reproducibility, or ethical considerations.

reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The proposed method is guaranteed by PAC bound. 

Weaknesses:
1. The paper is not presented in a good way. The notation is very confusing and misleading in general.
2. A big picture is missing. For example, we know that f is a pre-trained meta-model. But in Figure 1 this information is missing which may confuse the meta-learning people.
reviews.questions:
1. In terms of evaluation, I think both predictions set error and prediction set size are considered as the measurements. Among the experiment settings, Meta-PS shows relatively low set error but in some cases set size is larger than others. I think the idea case is that both prediction set error and prediction set size are small. So can the author draw a clear conclusion for the comparison between Meta-CP and Meta-PS?
2. The authors select PS-Binom to estimate \tau. Do different estimators make difference in the performance? 
3. The score functions in lines 108 and 106 have different input spaces. Can the author explain why? My understanding is that f is a meta-learned off-the-shelf model.

2. Only one meta-model, prototypical network, is applied as a score function to evaluate the proposed method. 
3. The notations are very confusing, especially those applied for the score function, and prediction set. 
[1] Park, Sangdon, et al. "PAC confidence sets for deep neural networks via calibrated prediction." arXiv preprint arXiv:2001.00106 (2019).

reviews.text:
# Peer Review (Rewritten)

## Summary

This work introduces an approach for constructing prediction sets during meta-testing by leveraging prediction sets obtained through meta-training adaptation and calibration procedures. The methodology is carefully constructed to align with PAC-learning principles, thereby establishing theoretical guarantees for generating meta-test prediction sets without requiring additional calibration. The proposed approach is evaluated across four datasets spanning three distinct application domains, yielding results that are competitive with existing methods.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

**Strengths:**
1. The method is underpinned by formal PAC-learning bounds, providing theoretical grounding.

**Weaknesses:**
1. The paper suffers from presentation issues. The mathematical notation is frequently confusing and sometimes misleading throughout the manuscript.
2. The paper lacks a clear overarching narrative. For instance, while f is described as a pre-trained meta-model, this critical detail is absent from Figure 1, which could be particularly problematic for readers familiar with meta-learning literature.

## Questions and Concerns

1. Regarding the experimental evaluation, both prediction set error and prediction set size serve as performance metrics. The results show that Meta-PS achieves relatively low set error in several cases, though its set size occasionally exceeds that of competing methods. Since the ideal scenario involves minimizing both metrics simultaneously, could the authors provide a more definitive assessment comparing Meta-CP and Meta-PS? A clearer discussion of this trade-off would strengthen the paper.

2. The authors employ PS-Binom as their estimator for τ. Have the authors investigated whether alternative estimators would yield substantially different performance characteristics?

3. The score functions presented at lines 108 and 106 operate over different input domains. The authors should clarify this discrepancy, particularly given that f is understood to be a meta-learned pre-existing model.

## Limitations

1. The paper is not sufficiently self-contained. Readers unfamiliar with reference [1] will encounter significant comprehension difficulties. The authors should substantially expand the background exposition to make the work more accessible.
2. The empirical validation relies on a single meta-model architecture—prototypical networks—as the score function. Evaluation across multiple meta-model variants would strengthen the generalizability claims.
3. The mathematical notation introduces considerable confusion, particularly regarding the definitions of score functions and prediction sets. Substantial notational clarification is warranted.

## Overall Assessment

**Rating: 5 (Borderline accept)**

This is a technically sound contribution where the merits of acceptance marginally outweigh the limitations, though the evaluation scope is restricted. The theoretical framework is solid, but the presentation requires improvement, and broader empirical validation would be beneficial. The work makes a meaningful contribution to the field despite these constraints.

title: Grounded Reinforcement Learning: Learning to Win the Game under Human Commands

reviews.summary:
Paper introduces a new task called “Grounded Reinforcement Learning” in which agents must be reward-maximizing with respect to some high-level language “constraint” (given by another agent/human). The authors demonstrate that existing vanilla RL, constrained RL (CRL), and behavior cloning (BC) fail to perform well both with and without commands in the MiniRTS environment (an example of this problem setup).

To solve this problem setting, the authors propose a new method RED - Reinforced Demonstration Distillation, which repeatedly alternates between pure RL and BC updates.  The authors demonstrate that RED is both the strongest and most grounded policy. The authors provide, through ablations, compelling arguments.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

Introduces (somewhat) of a new problem statement

Clearly written 

Human Evaluation is compelling

Impactful work from a safety perspective. 

Weaknesses:

My largest criticism is the use of the word "language" and i think this undermines the entire approach - the current representation treats commands as one-hot representations. These are not learnt, shaped or any different to the state space. This has no standard components which i would consider natural language, such as pragmatics, syntax and semantics.

RED algorithm does not seem novel (effectively mixture of two)

Results are not significantly better than baselines.
reviews.questions:
1) It is unclear how this approach is different from having standard RL with a combined state space over both command and game state.  As the command representation is not learnt - it seems unclear how this actually is different to a standard RL problem.

2) Can more justification be given to how difficult the opponent you play with is? Can you play with a harder agent to more clearly demonstrate the advantages of your method?

3) It is really unclear how the command token is meaningfully different to having an additional dimension within the game state. Can some justification be given to how either this representation is learnt or has any assemblance of language (is it compositional, have semantic or pragmatic properties).
reviews.limitations:
Addressing the above questions would be useful. 

I also believe actually using a language model would take great steps in demonstrating the effectiveness of your approach.

The authors demonstrate that existing vanilla RL, constrained RL (CRL), and behavior cloning (BC) fail to perform well both with and without commands in the MiniRTS environment (an example of this problem setup).

To solve this problem setting, the authors propose a new method RED - Reinforced Demonstration Distillation, which repeatedly alternates between pure RL and BC updates. Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:

Introduces (somewhat) of a new problem statement

Clearly written 

Human Evaluation is compelling

Impactful work from a safety perspective. 

Weaknesses:

My largest criticism is the use of the word "language" and i think this undermines the entire approach - the current representation treats commands as one-hot representations. These are not learnt, shaped or any different to the state space. This has no standard components which i would consider natural language, such as pragmatics, syntax and semantics.

Questions:

reviews.text:
# Peer Review

## Summary

This paper introduces Grounded Reinforcement Learning, a novel problem formulation where agents must maximize reward while adhering to high-level language-based constraints provided by humans or other agents. The authors evaluate existing approaches—standard RL, constrained RL, and behavior cloning—on the MiniRTS environment and demonstrate their inadequacy for this setting. To address this limitation, they propose RED (Reinforced Demonstration Distillation), an algorithm that iteratively combines RL optimization with behavioral cloning. Empirical results show RED achieves superior performance and better constraint adherence compared to baseline methods, supported by thorough ablation studies.

## Strengths

- **Novel Problem Formulation**: The paper articulates a meaningful and underexplored problem space that bridges language grounding and reinforcement learning
- **Clear Presentation**: The manuscript is well-written and easy to follow throughout
- **Strong Human Evaluation**: The human evaluation studies provide convincing evidence for the practical utility of the proposed approach
- **Safety Relevance**: The work addresses an important concern in AI safety and alignment, with clear implications for real-world deployment

## Weaknesses

**Primary Concern - Language Representation**:
The fundamental use of the term "language" is problematic and weakens the entire contribution. The current implementation represents commands as one-hot encodings that are not learned, transformed, or meaningfully distinguished from standard state dimensions. This encoding lacks essential properties of natural language—including pragmatics, syntax, and semantic structure. The terminology oversells the linguistic component of the work.

**Algorithm Novelty**:
RED appears to be a relatively straightforward combination of two existing techniques (RL and BC) without substantial algorithmic innovation. The alternating optimization scheme, while practical, does not constitute a significant methodological advance.

**Marginal Empirical Improvements**:
The quantitative performance gains over baseline methods are modest and do not substantially strengthen the empirical case for the approach.

## Questions and Concerns

1. **Conceptual Clarity**: How does this formulation fundamentally differ from standard RL with an expanded state space that includes both command and game state information? Given that command representations are not learned or transformed, the distinction from conventional RL appears unclear.

2. **Opponent Difficulty**: The paper lacks sufficient justification regarding the difficulty level of the opponent agents used in evaluation. Testing against stronger adversaries would more convincingly demonstrate the method's advantages and practical value.

3. **Language Grounding**: The command tokens appear functionally equivalent to additional state dimensions. Either the representation should be learned and transformed in a meaningful way, or the work should justify how it exhibits properties of genuine language such as compositionality, semantic content, or pragmatic interpretation.

## Recommendations for Improvement

The issues raised above should be directly addressed in a revision. Most importantly, incorporating actual language model components would substantially strengthen claims about language grounding and would better validate the approach's effectiveness in handling natural language constraints. This enhancement would also better justify the terminology and positioning of the work.

## Overall Assessment

This technically sound paper makes a reasonable contribution to an important problem area. The problem formulation is timely and relevant for AI safety research, and the empirical evaluation demonstrates competent experimental work. However, the modest algorithmic novelty, limited empirical gains, and particularly the misalignment between the paper's language-centric framing and its actual use of symbolic one-hot encodings present notable limitations. Despite these concerns, the work's relevance to safety-critical applications and its clear articulation of a previously underexplored problem setting justify acceptance.

## Rating

**7: Accept** – Technically solid paper with meaningful impact on an important sub-area (AI alignment and safety), supported by adequate evaluation, good reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical concerns. While the work has limitations in novelty and empirical margins, the problem formulation and safety implications warrant publication.

reviews.summary:
This work focuses on grounded reinforcement learning. Generally, it means finding a conditional RL policy that can perfectly response to high-level command. 

In this paper, the authors proposed a constraned RL algorithm RED to (1) train an agent that is capable of winning strategy games and (2) follow most human instructions. Concretely, RED uses RL to learn explorative policy while uses BC to imitate human demonstration.

Experiment results suggest RED achieves command-following policies and higher winning rate.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
### Strengths

* The idea is concise. Use BC to learn human behavior while use RL to explore better solution.
* Visualization and videos provided are mostly clear and contain necessary information.
* Fig1 is illustrative and helpful to explain what is grounded reinforcement learning. (It would be better if Fig 1 has no background color and in PDF format)
* Code is released.

### Weaknesses

* This work assumes reward from interactive environment. (limitation)
* The code has no detailed guidance and documentation.
* Though I like Figure 2 and other experiments, the main experiment results in Table 1 is confusing and non-convincing.
* The idea is not novel. Conditional imitation learning is a long lasting topics. Refer to "End-to-end Driving via Conditional Imitation Learning"
* Many details are missing or confusing, such as Table 1 and Lagrangian method.
reviews.questions:
### Questions on method


Sec 4.3 says this work uses Lagrangian multiplier to solve the constrained optimization problem in Eq2. It turns out that, in my understanding, this is simply a weighted loss. A formal Lagrangian method should contain update process of the $\beta$, which is just ignored by this paper (or I miss, please point out). The later "iterative solution" also breaks the so called "Lagrangian method". 

---

Line 240 and Appendix A.2: Could you discuss more details on how you deal with action space? What does the "0/1 action for each unit" means? Who does the selection?

---

How to insert a NA command? IIUC, there exist a fixed set of possible commands right? So NA command should be just one more command in that set. You create embedding for each command in the set and than pass the embedding to the neural network.

---





### Questions on experiments

I can't understand table 1. IIUC, these four baselines have the policies that do not take command as input. Why they have different performance in Table 1 when applying different test commands? Please describe how you turn them as conditional policies. 

For example, since RL is trained without any objective to conduct distinguishable behaviors under different commands, why inserting different test commands will lead to different behavior?

---

I don't think Table 1 is convincing, especially when taking STD into account. 

Line 288: "Both RED and Joint policies substantially outperform Switch (BC) policy with sub-optimal commands (Random and Human Proxy), suggesting the conditioned behaviors are improved." This is a overclaimed statement. I can't draw this conclusion from Table 1.

---

I like Fig 2, which shows the dependency between commands and behaviors. Many "conditional RL" methods fail to prove that the conditional policy has casuality to the input command. This figure also shows that RED has much better "casuality" than RL.

My problem is that, in Figure 2, RL in 0% random commands achieves ~57% win rate. Does this value align with the RL performance with NA test command in Table 1?

Again, if RL is trained compelety without human command, then (1) where you can insert the command to the policy? and (2) why RL performance changes with the percentage of random commands?

---


Figure 6 says RL policy achieves similar win rate compared to RED. IIUC, RL policies are not conditioned on any human command. This result suggest that a human subject cooperates with RED trained conditional policy will lead to RL-comparable performance? This seems to be a violation to Table 1 "Oracle" result, which might lead to 

---

There is only one real gameplay footage provided (less than 2 minutes), with only 6 commands given, which is far less than convincing. Meanwhile, the commands seemed to be programmed/copy-pasted (I did not see any input lag), is there any real human intervention involved in recoding this video?

---

This game seems to be very complicated. Does your first intention of the application of this algorithm to be this high-level game? I understand the miniRTS environment is the only one that fitted the algorithm's need, but if you have alternatives (even if the performance is weak), would you also like to present them in the paper? Having diversity is a huge plus to your paper.








### Questions on paper




### Other questions or concerns



The [google drive link](https://drive.google.com/file/d/129ZV9CU6zUnp5B4v0p91Xaae8-M5zxHy/view) provided is **not anonymous**. This is a violation of the NeurIPS Code of Conduct (double-blind reviewing policy). Please action ASAP.

---

This paper has experiments involving 30 college students experiments, are attendees aware of their rights and responsibilities? A more explicit Ethics Statement should be included.

---

Conditional BC is not a new idea. Please refer to this work: End-to-end Driving via Conditional Imitation Learning

---

Appendix D, as said in the checklist, has no information on human experiment. You moved them to Appendix E.

---

The website given in the appendix is very informative and I like the real-time gameplay footage, but there are several problems I want to point out:
- Code link is broken.
- Many grammar errors, will not be present here (plural or singular, tense, etc)
reviews.limitations:
The authors provide limitation section.

GRL is an interesting field. How to improve human-AI cooperation in test-time, how to learn a better human proxy, and how to further encourage, illustrate, quantify the causity between command and behaviors might be future directions.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Summary

This paper addresses grounded reinforcement learning, which involves training a conditional RL policy capable of responding appropriately to high-level human commands. The authors introduce RED, a constrained reinforcement learning algorithm designed to achieve two objectives: (1) enable an agent to develop winning strategies in competitive games, and (2) maintain adherence to human instructions. The approach combines behavior cloning to learn from human demonstrations with reinforcement learning to discover improved policies. Experimental findings demonstrate that RED successfully produces command-responsive policies while maintaining competitive win rates.

## Strengths

* The core concept is straightforward and intuitive: leveraging behavior cloning to capture human behavior patterns while simultaneously employing RL to identify superior strategies.
* The paper includes clear visualizations and supplementary video materials that effectively communicate the work's contributions.
* Figure 1 provides an excellent conceptual illustration of grounded reinforcement learning and aids reader comprehension. (Note: The figure would benefit from removing background colors and being presented in PDF format.)
* The authors have made their implementation publicly available.

## Weaknesses

* The approach relies on obtaining reward signals from the interactive environment, which represents a significant practical limitation.
* The released code lacks comprehensive documentation and guidance for users attempting to reproduce or build upon this work.
* While Figure 2 and supporting experimental analyses are compelling, the primary results presented in Table 1 are ambiguous and fail to provide convincing evidence for the main claims.
* The fundamental concept lacks novelty, as conditional imitation learning has been extensively studied in prior work, particularly in "End-to-end Driving via Conditional Imitation Learning."
* Several critical technical details remain unclear or insufficiently explained, including the interpretation of Table 1 results and the application of the Lagrangian method.

## Technical Concerns

**On the Constrained Optimization Approach:**
Section 4.3 describes employing Lagrangian multipliers to address the constrained optimization problem in Equation 2. However, the implementation appears to reduce this to a simple weighted loss combination. A rigorous Lagrangian approach would necessitate an explicit update mechanism for the parameter β, which appears absent from the presentation (or may have been overlooked in my reading). Furthermore, the subsequent "iterative solution" seems inconsistent with standard Lagrangian methodology.

**On Action Space Handling:**
Lines 240 and Appendix A.2 require elaboration regarding action space management. The description of "0/1 action for each unit" remains unclear. Could the authors clarify the selection mechanism and how this representation functions within the overall architecture?

**On Command Incorporation:**
The mechanism for introducing a null/NA command needs clarification. If a fixed command vocabulary exists, should the NA command simply constitute an additional entry within this set, with corresponding embeddings passed to the network?

## Experimental Questions

**On Table 1 Interpretation:**
Table 1 presents significant interpretive challenges. If the four baseline policies were trained without command conditioning, how can they exhibit varying performance across different test command scenarios? The paper should explicitly describe the procedure for converting non-conditional policies into conditional variants. Specifically, since the RL baseline lacks any objective to generate command-dependent behaviors, what mechanism explains the performance variation when different test commands are applied?

**On Result Convincingness:**
Table 1 fails to provide compelling evidence, particularly when considering standard deviations. The claim in Line 288 that "Both RED and Joint policies substantially outperform Switch (BC) policy with sub-optimal commands (Random and Human Proxy), suggesting the conditioned behaviors are improved" appears overstated given the table's contents and cannot be reliably inferred from the reported numbers.

**On Command-Behavior Causality:**
Figure 2 effectively demonstrates the dependency between commands and resulting behaviors, addressing a common weakness in conditional RL approaches where causality between inputs and outputs remains unestablished. The figure convincingly shows RED's superior command-responsiveness compared to the RL baseline. However, a discrepancy requires resolution: the RL policy achieves approximately 57% win rate with 0% random commands in Figure 2. Does this align with the RL performance under NA command conditions reported in Table 1? Additionally, if RL training completely omits human commands, how can commands be inserted into the policy at test time, and why would the proportion of random commands during testing affect performance?

**On Figure 6 Consistency:**
Figure 6 indicates that the RL policy achieves comparable win rates to RED. Given that RL policies lack command conditioning, does this suggest that human operators cooperating with RED-trained conditional policies would achieve RL-equivalent performance? This interpretation appears contradictory to Table 1's oracle results and warrants clarification.

**On Real-World Validation:**
The paper provides only minimal real gameplay footage (under two minutes) featuring merely six commands—insufficient for convincing validation. Additionally, the commands appear to be pre-programmed or templated rather than genuinely human-controlled (no observable input latency is evident). The authors should clarify whether authentic human intervention was involved in generating this footage.

**On Experimental Scope:**
Given the algorithm's complexity, the focus on a single high-complexity game appears limiting. While miniRTS may be the only environment meeting the algorithm's specific requirements, presenting results on alternative environments—even if performance is suboptimal—would substantially strengthen the paper's generalizability claims.

## Administrative and Ethical Concerns

* The Google Drive link provided in supplementary materials is **not anonymized**, constituting a violation of NeurIPS's double-blind review policy. This requires immediate correction.

* The paper describes experiments involving 30 college student participants. An explicit Ethics Statement addressing participant awareness of their rights and responsibilities should be included.

* Conditional behavior cloning is not a novel contribution. Prior work, particularly "End-to-end Driving via Conditional Imitation Learning," should be acknowledged as establishing this paradigm.

* Appendix D lacks information on human experiments as indicated in the checklist; this content appears relocated to Appendix E.

* While the supplementary website provides valuable real-time gameplay demonstrations, several issues are evident: the code link is non-functional, and numerous grammatical errors appear throughout (inconsistent pluralization, tense agreement, etc.).

## Limitations

The authors appropriately acknowledge limitations in their work. Future research directions could productively focus on enhancing human-AI cooperation at test time, developing more sophisticated human behavior models, and establishing more rigorous quantitative measures of command-behavior causality.

## Overall Assessment

This work addresses an interesting problem in grounded reinforcement learning. The technical approach is fundamentally sound, and the paper makes meaningful contributions to the field. However, the experimental validation, particularly Table 1, requires substantial clarification and appears insufficiently convincing in its current form. The novelty is moderate given prior work on conditional imitation learning. Despite these concerns, the work demonstrates technical competence and provides useful insights for the community.

---

## Recommendation

**6: Weak Accept** — The paper presents technically sound methodology with moderate-to-high potential impact. While experimental validation could be strengthened and certain technical details require clarification, there are no fundamental concerns regarding evaluation rigor, resource availability, reproducibility, or ethical considerations that would warrant rejection.

This seems to be a violation to Table 1 "Oracle" result, which might lead to 

---

There is only one real gameplay footage provided (less than 2 minutes), with only 6 commands given, which is far less than convincing. Meanwhile, the commands seemed to be programmed/copy-pasted (I did not see any input lag), is there any real human intervention involved in recoding this video?

---

This game seems to be very complicated. Does your first intention of the application of this algorithm to be this high-level game? I understand the miniRTS environment is the only one that fitted the algorithm's need, but if you have alternatives (even if the performance is weak), would you also like to present them in the paper? Having diversity is a huge plus to your paper.








### Questions on paper




### Other questions or concerns



The [google drive link](https://drive.google.com/file/d/129ZV9CU6zUnp5B4v0p91Xaae8-M5zxHy/view) provided is **not anonymous**. This is a violation of the NeurIPS Code of Conduct (double-blind reviewing policy). Please action ASAP.

---

This paper has experiments involving 30 college students experiments, are attendees aware of their rights and responsibilities? A more explicit Ethics Statement should be included.

---

Conditional BC is not a new idea. Please refer to this work: End-to-end Driving via Conditional Imitation Learning

---

Appendix D, as said in the checklist, has no information on human experiment. You moved them to Appendix E.

---

The website given in the appendix is very informative and I like the real-time gameplay footage, but there are several problems I want to point out:
- Code link is broken.
- Many grammar errors, will not be present here (plural or singular, tense, etc)

Limitations:
The authors provide limitation section.

GRL is an interesting field. How to improve human-AI cooperation in test-time, how to learn a better human proxy, and how to further encourage, illustrate, quantify the causity between command and behaviors might be future directions.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
