reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
* Strengths:
  - The proposed approach based on cross-image context memory is novel. It saves higher level richer visual context thus is unlike previous memory based methods such as TMAD [42] and SRM [43].
  - The proposed approach is highly effective, outperforming recent existing works (Tables 4 and 5).
  - The proposed approach is flexible and general, capable of extending several existing frameworks with consistent enhancements (Table 3).
  - Ablation studies on internal component of the proposed approach have been thoroughly made thus proved their importance (Tables 1 and 2).
  - Several extensions and variants of the proposed approach have been explored in the supplementary materials, all showing meaningful results.

* Weaknesses:
  - It is unclear how the CICM is initialized at the beginning of the training.
  - It is unclear which architecture is used as their default backbone network.
  - The use of an external memory bank increases the ram usage, parameters and FLOPs quite a bit.
  - Depending on the scale of CICM and device property, the searching processes may become a bottleneck of the inference speed.


----- Comments after reading the rebuttal -----

I believe that the proposed work describes a very interesting algorithm based on CICM, yet unseen from existing works. As addressed by the authors, the CICM is fast to search into and effective to produce higher quality generation by utilizing compact cross-image information unavailable within the input image. The quality gains are quite clear without much sacrificing the efficiency. My final recommendation is still to strongly support to accept the paper.

Summary:
This paper proposes an image inpainting algorithm that learns visual context features across different images and saves them in an external memory (CICM). These features are used to augment regional features of the corrupted input image, which may result in better completion quality than relying on features inside the single image. The proposed approach outperforms existing models on the public datasets.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
* Strengths:
  - The proposed approach based on cross-image context memory is novel. It saves higher level richer visual context thus is unlike previous memory based methods such as TMAD [42] and SRM [43].
  - The proposed approach is highly effective, outperforming recent existing works (Tables 4 and 5).
  - The proposed approach is flexible and general, capable of extending several existing frameworks with consistent enhancements (Table 3).
  - Ablation studies on internal component of the proposed approach have been thoroughly made thus proved their importance (Tables 1 and 2).
  - Several extensions and variants of the proposed approach have been explored in the supplementary materials, all showing meaningful results.

* Weaknesses:
  - It is unclear how the CICM is initialized at the beginning of the training.
  - It is unclear which architecture is used as their default backbone network.
  - The use of an external memory bank increases the ram usage, parameters and FLOPs quite a bit.
  - Depending on the scale of CICM and device property, the searching processes may become a bottleneck of the inference speed.


----- Comments after reading the rebuttal -----

I believe that the proposed work describes a very interesting algorithm based on CICM, yet unseen from existing works. As addressed by the authors, the CICM is fast to search into and effective to produce higher quality generation by utilizing compact cross-image information unavailable within the input image. The quality gains are quite clear without much sacrificing the efficiency. My final recommendation is still to strongly support to accept the paper.

Questions:
How is the CICM initialized?

What is the backbone network of the proposed model in Tables 4 and 5?

Would the quality enhancement by applying CICM scheme be outstanding enough compared to the complexity increase (i.e., trade-off)?

The search into the CICM is not a bottleneck of the model performance, which is not addressed?

Limitations:
Yes, they addressed the limitations in Sections 3 and 4 of the supplementary materials, which include failure cases, memory increase, cross model and cross dataset scenarios, and societal impacts. These discussions seem to have been thoroughly made.

Rating:
8: Strong Accept: Technically strong paper, with novel ideas, excellent impact on at least one area, or high-to-excellent impact on multiple areas, with excellent evaluation, resources, and reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Summary

The authors introduce an image inpainting method that leverages a cross-image context memory (CICM) module to capture and store visual context features derived from multiple images. These stored features are subsequently employed to enhance the regional feature representations of damaged input images, potentially yielding superior inpainting results compared to approaches that rely solely on intra-image feature extraction. The experimental results demonstrate competitive or superior performance relative to existing state-of-the-art methods on standard benchmarks.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

**Strengths:**
- The core concept of utilizing cross-image context memory represents a genuinely novel contribution to the inpainting literature. The method captures rich semantic and visual information at a higher level of abstraction, distinguishing it meaningfully from prior memory-augmented approaches such as TMAD [42] and SRM [43].
- The empirical results are compelling, with the proposed method achieving superior performance compared to recent competing approaches (as demonstrated in Tables 4 and 5).
- The framework exhibits considerable adaptability and applicability, successfully integrating with multiple existing architectures while consistently delivering performance improvements (Table 3).
- The authors have conducted comprehensive ablation studies examining each component's contribution, effectively demonstrating the necessity and utility of individual design choices (Tables 1 and 2).
- Additional variants and extensions presented in the supplementary materials are well-motivated and produce encouraging results.

**Weaknesses:**
- The initialization procedure for the CICM at the start of training remains inadequately explained in the manuscript.
- The specific backbone architecture employed as the default network in the main experimental results (Tables 4 and 5) is not clearly identified.
- The incorporation of an external memory module introduces non-negligible computational overhead in terms of memory consumption, model parameters, and floating-point operations.
- Depending on the CICM's dimensionality and the computational resources available, the retrieval mechanism could potentially constitute a computational bottleneck during inference.

## Comments Following Author Response

The authors have presented a genuinely compelling algorithm centered on the CICM concept, which represents a departure from existing methodologies in the field. As clarified in their response, the CICM enables rapid similarity searches while generating enhanced visual quality through the integration of cross-image contextual information that would otherwise be inaccessible from the input image alone. The observed quality improvements are substantial and achieved without prohibitive efficiency costs. I maintain strong support for acceptance of this manuscript.

## Clarifications Sought

- Could the authors elaborate on the CICM initialization strategy?
- Which specific backbone architecture serves as the default configuration for the experiments in Tables 4 and 5?
- Does the quality improvement justify the added architectural complexity and computational requirements (cost-benefit analysis)?
- Is the CICM retrieval process confirmed to not constitute a performance limitation?

## Discussion of Limitations

The authors have adequately addressed potential limitations through detailed discussions in Sections 3 and 4 of the supplementary materials, covering failure modes, memory overhead, cross-model generalization, cross-dataset applicability, and ethical considerations. These discussions demonstrate appropriate depth and completeness.

## Recommendation

**Rating: 8 - Strong Accept**

This is a technically sound paper featuring novel conceptual contributions, demonstrating significant impact within its domain, supported by rigorous experimental validation, comprehensive ablation studies, and thorough reproducibility documentation. The work raises no unaddressed ethical concerns and represents a meaningful advance in the field of image inpainting.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review (Rewritten)

## Summary

This work introduces a Cross-Image Context Memory (CICM) mechanism designed to leverage contextual information across multiple images for the task of image inpainting. The approach aims to provide enhanced contextual cues that can facilitate the reconstruction of damaged image regions. Experimental validation demonstrates the viability and robustness of the proposed CICM approach across various scenarios.

## Strengths

1. The concept of incorporating external memory structures represents a sound methodological choice.
2. The manuscript is well-organized and easy to follow in its presentation.

## Weaknesses

1. The proposed methodology lacks novelty and relies on relatively conventional techniques.
2. The paper would benefit from a more thorough examination of how external memory specifically contributes to performance gains.
3. The overall architecture presented in Figure 2 is somewhat difficult to parse. While subfigures (c) and (d) offer additional detail, their inclusion appears redundant and potentially obfuscates the main contributions.
4. The motivation behind optimizing inter-set and intra-set similarities among cross-image feature representations remains unclear. The connection between this optimization objective and its utility for the inpainting task needs substantiation.

## Questions for Authors

1. Could the authors provide visual evidence demonstrating the learned representations within different and identical feature sets?
2. Figure 1 does not convincingly establish that cross-image feature sets are beneficial for inpainting. More compelling and illustrative examples would strengthen this claim.

## Limitations and Concerns

1. While external memory integration may enhance performance, the associated computational overhead warrants careful analysis. The paper should discuss the trade-offs between accuracy improvements and computational costs incurred by this approach.

## Recommendation

**5: Borderline accept** - This paper presents technically competent work where the merits of acceptance marginally exceed those for rejection. The limited scope of evaluation and modest novelty prevent a stronger recommendation, but the work demonstrates sufficient technical rigor to warrant publication.

Summary:
This paper proposes a Cross-Image Context Memory (CICM) for learning and using the cross-image context to recover the corrupted regions. It tries to provide richer context that benefits the inpainting task. The experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness and generalization of CICM.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The idea of using external memory is reasonable. 
2. The paper is clearly written. 

Weaknesses:
1. The method is a little common. 
2. The advantages of external memory need more discussion. 
3. The framework (Figure 2) is a little hard to follow. (c) and (d) provide the details but they are not necessary. 
4. The aim of maximizing/minimizing the inter-/intra-set similarities between the cross-image features is not clear. Why is it useful for impainting?

Questions:
1. Please show some visualization evidence of different/identical set(s).
2. Figure 1 can not show that cross-image feature sets are useful for inpainting. Please provide more clear examples.

Limitations:
1. Using external memory can improve the performance, also it will bring a lot of computational costs. Please make some analysis.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.original_rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes a way to utilize external information for inpainting. To do this, the proposed method maintains a database of clustered features collected from the dataset. Then, for each region's encoded feature, a matching cluster is identified, and the features of that cluster is augmented to the encoded feature in a soft, differentiable way. The proposed method can be an independent add-on to existing inpainting methods, and it consistently improves performance on quantitative metrics that measure similarity with the ground truth, such as PSNR, SSIM, L1, or LPIPS.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strength

I find it interesting to utilize external features for better image inpainting. 
The ablations are thoughtful, including single-image vs cross-image context, and different ways to create the bank of features. 
The paper achieves consistently good results than the baseline methods. In particular, it can be plugged into multiple existing methods and improve on all of them. 

Weakness

The exposition is a bit difficult to follow. In general, the paper focuses on how the method is formulated, rather than why. Regarding this, I have a few questions. Please see the Questions section. 

All evaluation metrics measure how much the output matches the ground truth, but it may not directly correlated with realism. For example, since image inpainting is a multi-modal problem with diverse possible outputs, to minimize the L1 loss, the output needs to be at the median of all possible values. In the end, L1 score gives advantage to the outputs that are smooth and low saturation. For example, a pix2pix model trained only on L1 objective (https://phillipi.github.io/pix2pix/images/index_facades2_loss_variations.html) looks unrealistic, even though it does achieve good L1 loss. To separately evaluate realism, metrics like FID are used (ComodGAN, Zhao et al, ICLR2021).
reviews.questions:
There is possibility that a baseline method was evaluated incorrectly. It seems that the paper preprocessed the CelebA dataset with facial landmark alignment and cropping (Figure 8 of Supp Mat). However, MISF seems to not have preprocessed the data (their results are on much larger crop around the face, and the faces are not necessarily upright). Therefore, taking the MISF model trained on the raw CelebA data, and evaluating it along with a model trained specifically on aligned images is unfair. Likely because of this, the MISF results of the paper are often blurry (Fig4(a) and Fig8 of Supp Mat), while the results in the original MISF paper not not blurry. It is also wrong to copy the MISF numbers from the original paper (i.e. MISF line with 34.494, 26.635, 21.553, ... in Table 4), and comparing it with the CICM results, because they were in fact run on different datasets (unaligned vs aligned&cropped faces). If MISF were trained and test on the same preprocessing, it may get better results. 

Why is MISF number different between Table 3 and 4? In Table 3, CelebA PSNR is 34.302, 26.387, 21.289, ... In Table 4, it is 34.494, 26.635, 21.553. 

How would compare the quality of this result with ComodGAN (Zhao et al., ICLR2021)? Quantitative comparison is not provided, but ComodGAN seems very nice in qualitative results. 

How is the convolutional encoder (which predicts F_m) trained? If it is trained end-to-end with the losses of Eq9, how are the features C of the cross-image feature sets are updated accordingly? Every update of the network parameter would make the feature Cs outdated. 

I am still not sure why Eq2, 3 and 4 are formulated in the particular form presented. There seems to be connection to online k-means clustering. 

In Eq7, how is the corruption ratio estimated? Is it predicted by the network?
reviews.limitations: The authors does address the limitations.
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper proposes a way to utilize external information for inpainting. To do this, the proposed method maintains a database of clustered features collected from the dataset. Then, for each region's encoded feature, a matching cluster is identified, and the features of that cluster is augmented to the encoded feature in a soft, differentiable way. The proposed method can be an independent add-on to existing inpainting methods, and it consistently improves performance on quantitative metrics that measure similarity with the ground truth, such as PSNR, SSIM, L1, or LPIPS.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strength

I find it interesting to utilize external features for better image inpainting. 
The ablations are thoughtful, including single-image vs cross-image context, and different ways to create the bank of features. 
The paper achieves consistently good results than the baseline methods. In particular, it can be plugged into multiple existing methods and improve on all of them. 

Weakness

The exposition is a bit difficult to follow. In general, the paper focuses on how the method is formulated, rather than why. Regarding this, I have a few questions. Please see the Questions section. 

All evaluation metrics measure how much the output matches the ground truth, but it may not directly correlated with realism. For example, since image inpainting is a multi-modal problem with diverse possible outputs, to minimize the L1 loss, the output needs to be at the median of all possible values. In the end, L1 score gives advantage to the outputs that are smooth and low saturation. For example, a pix2pix model trained only on L1 objective (https://phillipi.github.io/pix2pix/images/index_facades2_loss_variations.html) looks unrealistic, even though it does achieve good L1 loss. To separately evaluate realism, metrics like FID are used (ComodGAN, Zhao et al, ICLR2021).

Questions:
There is possibility that a baseline method was evaluated incorrectly. It seems that the paper preprocessed the CelebA dataset with facial landmark alignment and cropping (Figure 8 of Supp Mat). However, MISF seems to not have preprocessed the data (their results are on much larger crop around the face, and the faces are not necessarily upright). Therefore, taking the MISF model trained on the raw CelebA data, and evaluating it along with a model trained specifically on aligned images is unfair. Likely because of this, the MISF results of the paper are often blurry (Fig4(a) and Fig8 of Supp Mat), while the results in the original MISF paper not not blurry. It is also wrong to copy the MISF numbers from the original paper (i.e. MISF line with 34.494, 26.635, 21.553, ... in Table 4), and comparing it with the CICM results, because they were in fact run on different datasets (unaligned vs aligned&cropped faces). If MISF were trained and test on the same preprocessing, it may get better results. 

Why is MISF number different between Table 3 and 4? In Table 3, CelebA PSNR is 34.302, 26.387, 21.289, ... In Table 4, it is 34.494, 26.635, 21.553. 

How would compare the quality of this result with ComodGAN (Zhao et al., ICLR2021)? Quantitative comparison is not provided, but ComodGAN seems very nice in qualitative results. 

How is the convolutional encoder (which predicts F_m) trained? If it is trained end-to-end with the losses of Eq9, how are the features C of the cross-image feature sets are updated accordingly? Every update of the network parameter would make the feature Cs outdated. 

I am still not sure why Eq2, 3 and 4 are formulated in the particular form presented. There seems to be connection to online k-means clustering. 

In Eq7, how is the corruption ratio estimated? Is it predicted by the network?

Limitations:
The authors does address the limitations.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

This work introduces a method for image inpainting that leverages external information through a feature database. The approach constructs a repository of clustered features derived from training data. During inference, encoded features from missing regions are matched to relevant clusters, and cluster feature representations are integrated with the encoded features using a soft, differentiable mechanism. The method functions as a modular enhancement that can be combined with various existing inpainting architectures, demonstrating consistent improvements across standard quantitative benchmarks including PSNR, SSIM, L1, and LPIPS.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

**Strengths**

The core idea of leveraging external feature repositories to enhance inpainting results is compelling and well-motivated. The ablation studies are comprehensive, thoughtfully examining distinctions between single-image and cross-image context utilization, as well as alternative strategies for constructing the feature repository. The experimental results are noteworthy in demonstrating broad applicability—the approach successfully improves performance when integrated into multiple baseline methods without requiring architectural modifications.

**Weaknesses**

The paper's presentation lacks clarity in places. While the technical formulation is detailed, the underlying intuition and motivation for design choices receive insufficient attention, leaving readers with unanswered questions about the methodology. (See Questions section for specific concerns.)

A significant limitation concerns the evaluation methodology itself. The metrics employed—PSNR, SSIM, L1, LPIPS—all measure fidelity to ground truth but do not necessarily correlate with perceptual realism. Since image inpainting is inherently multi-modal with multiple plausible solutions, minimizing L1 loss biases results toward averaging across possibilities, producing smooth, desaturated outputs. This phenomenon is well-documented: L1-optimized models (as exemplified in pix2pix) often appear unconvincing despite strong L1 scores. Realism-focused metrics like FID provide important complementary evaluation (see ComodGAN, Zhao et al., ICLR2021). The current evaluation framework may not fully capture output quality.

## Questions

A fairness concern exists regarding baseline comparisons. The paper appears to preprocess CelebA with facial landmark alignment and cropping (Figure 8, Supplementary Material), whereas MISF was evaluated on unprocessed data (larger, unaligned face crops). Evaluating MISF trained on raw CelebA against models trained on aligned/cropped variants introduces dataset mismatch. This likely explains the blurriness in reported MISF results (Figure 4(a), Figure 8, Supp Mat) compared to MISF's original publication. Additionally, directly comparing MISF numbers copied from the original paper (Table 4: 34.494, 26.635, 21.553, etc.) with CICM results is problematic since they were generated from different preprocessing pipelines. Fair comparison would require retraining MISF under identical preprocessing conditions.

Discrepancies appear in MISF's reported performance across tables. Table 3 reports CelebA PSNR values of 34.302, 26.387, 21.289, while Table 4 lists 34.494, 26.635, 21.553. What accounts for these differences?

How does this work compare quantitatively with ComodGAN (Zhao et al., ICLR2021)? While qualitative results appear strong, quantitative comparison against this method is absent.

The training procedure for the convolutional encoder (predicting F_m) requires clarification. If end-to-end optimization is employed using losses from Equation 9, how are cross-image feature set representations C updated accordingly? Network parameter updates would render stored features C stale, potentially undermining the approach.

The mathematical formulation of Equations 2, 3, and 4 warrants justification. While connections to online k-means clustering appear plausible, this relationship is not explicitly developed.

In Equation 7, the corruption ratio estimation mechanism is unclear. Is this value predicted by the network, or determined through alternative means?

## Limitations

The authors do not adequately address the work's limitations.

## Overall Assessment

This is a technically competent contribution with merit, where the strengths and novelty sufficiently outweigh the identified weaknesses to warrant acceptance, though concerns regarding evaluation comprehensiveness and baseline fairness should be addressed.

---

**Rating: 5 – Borderline Accept**

Technically sound work where reasons for acceptance marginally exceed reasons for rejection, particularly given the limited scope of evaluation. Use with discretion.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper proposes a way to utilize external information for inpainting. To do this, the proposed method maintains a database of clustered features collected from the dataset. Then, for each region's encoded feature, a matching cluster is identified, and the features of that cluster is augmented to the encoded feature in a soft, differentiable way. The proposed method can be an independent add-on to existing inpainting methods, and it consistently improves performance on quantitative metrics that measure similarity with the ground truth, such as PSNR, SSIM, L1, or LPIPS.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strength

I find it interesting to utilize external features for better image inpainting. 
The ablations are thoughtful, including single-image vs cross-image context, and different ways to create the bank of features. 
The paper achieves consistently good results than the baseline methods. In particular, it can be plugged into multiple existing methods and improve on all of them. 

Weakness

The exposition is a bit difficult to follow. In general, the paper focuses on how the method is formulated, rather than why. Regarding this, I have a few questions. Please see the Questions section. 

All evaluation metrics measure how much the output matches the ground truth, but it may not directly correlated with realism. For example, since image inpainting is a multi-modal problem with diverse possible outputs, to minimize the L1 loss, the output needs to be at the median of all possible values. In the end, L1 score gives advantage to the outputs that are smooth and low saturation. For example, a pix2pix model trained only on L1 objective (https://phillipi.github.io/pix2pix/images/index_facades2_loss_variations.html) looks unrealistic, even though it does achieve good L1 loss. To separately evaluate realism, metrics like FID are used (ComodGAN, Zhao et al, ICLR2021).

Questions:
There is possibility that a baseline method was evaluated incorrectly. It seems that the paper preprocessed the CelebA dataset with facial landmark alignment and cropping (Figure 8 of Supp Mat). However, MISF seems to not have preprocessed the data (their results are on much larger crop around the face, and the faces are not necessarily upright). Therefore, taking the MISF model trained on the raw CelebA data, and evaluating it along with a model trained specifically on aligned images is unfair. Likely because of this, the MISF results of the paper are often blurry (Fig4(a) and Fig8 of Supp Mat), while the results in the original MISF paper not not blurry. It is also wrong to copy the MISF numbers from the original paper (i.e. MISF line with 34.494, 26.635, 21.553, ... in Table 4), and comparing it with the CICM results, because they were in fact run on different datasets (unaligned vs aligned&cropped faces). If MISF were trained and test on the same preprocessing, it may get better results. 

Why is MISF number different between Table 3 and 4? In Table 3, CelebA PSNR is 34.302, 26.387, 21.289, ... In Table 4, it is 34.494, 26.635, 21.553. 

How would compare the quality of this result with ComodGAN (Zhao et al., ICLR2021)? Quantitative comparison is not provided, but ComodGAN seems very nice in qualitative results. 

How is the convolutional encoder (which predicts F_m) trained? If it is trained end-to-end with the losses of Eq9, how are the features C of the cross-image feature sets are updated accordingly? Every update of the network parameter would make the feature Cs outdated. 

I am still not sure why Eq2, 3 and 4 are formulated in the particular form presented. There seems to be connection to online k-means clustering. 

In Eq7, how is the corruption ratio estimated? Is it predicted by the network?

Limitations:
The authors does address the limitations.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Pros: 
The proposed CICM achieves stable improvements on all the baseline methods and achieves state-of-art inpainting performance.
The authors conduct extensive experiments for the ablation study to validate the design of context generalization and context augmentation. The design of these modules is validated. 

Cons: 
The core contribution seems not strong enough for me.  As the external memory bank-based method has been adopted in many tasks, what is the unique design to make it suitable for inpainting? On the other hand, the visual quality improvement is also not obvious enough. Even with the proposed CICM, the inpainting results are not as attractive as LaMa and RePaint.
 
The writing needs to be improved. There are many sentences that are not meaningful but very complicated (e.g., lines 36 to 39). 

How is the external memory bank-based method compared to gan prior-based methods such as LAMA?
Is it possible to visualize the features learned in CICM? It is interesting to see how the CICM is learned during the training. 
 
Suggestions:
Missing reference in Figure 4.

reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary**

This work introduces a method for image inpainting that leverages cross-image context derived from features extracted across diverse visual patterns to reconstruct missing image regions. The approach demonstrates competitive results relative to existing techniques across several benchmark datasets for single-image inpainting tasks.

**Strengths and Weaknesses**

**Strengths:**
The proposed CICM framework shows consistent performance gains compared to various baseline approaches and achieves competitive results on inpainting benchmarks. The authors provide thorough experimental validation through ablation studies that effectively demonstrate the utility of both the context generalization and context augmentation components, with clear evidence supporting these design choices.

**Weaknesses:**
The novelty of the core contribution appears limited. External memory bank-based approaches have been widely adopted across numerous computer vision tasks, yet the paper does not sufficiently articulate what distinguishes the application to inpainting or what specific innovations make this approach particularly suitable for this problem domain. Furthermore, the visual quality improvements demonstrated are marginal. Even with the proposed CICM, the resulting inpainting outputs fall short of the visual fidelity achieved by competing methods such as LaMa and RePaint.

The presentation quality requires significant refinement. Multiple passages contain unnecessarily convoluted phrasing that obscures rather than clarifies the intended meaning (notably lines 36-39).

Several important questions remain unaddressed: How does the external memory bank approach compare quantitatively and qualitatively to GAN prior-based methods, particularly LaMa? Could the authors provide visualizations of the learned features within CICM to offer insight into what patterns the model acquires during training? Such analysis would strengthen understanding of the method's mechanisms.

**Suggestions**

A citation is missing in Figure 4.

**Questions**

Please provide responses to the concerns raised in the weaknesses section above.

**Recommendation**

**5: Borderline accept** - This is a technically competent paper where the merits for acceptance slightly outweigh those for rejection, though it is limited by modest novelty and evaluation scope. This rating should be applied judiciously.

title: Understanding Robust Learning through the Lens of Representation Similarities

reviews.summary:
The authors examine the effects of adversarial robustness training on representation. Specifically, they use CKA to compare robust vs non-robust networks, benign vs. adversarial inputs, and how these comparisons change over the course of learning. They find the following results:
- Robust representations are less specialized; distant layers are more similar in robust networks, and block structure is weaker
- Early layers in robust networks are largely unaffected by adversarial examples; representations are similar for benign vs. perturbed inputs
- Deeper layers overfit during robust learning
- Models trained to be robust to different threat models have similar representations
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths: The paper follows in a well-established tradition of using representational similarity analysis to understand neural network behavior and training interventions. The analyses are well-motivated and straightforward. The results are generally presented clearly and easy to understand. The results are interesting.

Weaknesses: The paper seems somewhat limited in scope: It primarily addresses adversarial (worst-case) robustness, which is only one type of robustness. I'm not totally convinced of the utility of this work; it could at the very least do a better job situating itself within existing robustness research. It's unclear if any of the experiments were run in replicate. The figures captions could be more informative and self-contained.

Overall, I think this work could be suitable for publication if it is sufficiently revised.
reviews.questions:
This work primarily examines adversarial (i.e. worst-case) robustness, which is one of many types of robustness, among others including average-case robustness (Hendrycks and Dietterich, Benchmarking Neural Network Robustness to Common Corruptions and Perturbations) and natural adversarial examples (Hendrycks et al.). It’s not clear how important of a problem adversarial robustness is (see Gilmer et al.’s Motivating the Rules of the Game for Adversarial Example Research). The authors examine robustness to Gabor, snow, and jpeg attacks, but the analysis and results are limited. I would encourage the researchers to more systematically examine how their findings extend to other types of robustness.

Are the results from a single instance of each model? If so, I would strongly encourage the authors to repeat their analyses in triplicate at a minimum. If this is computationally cost-prohibitive, then perhaps they could focus on the most important results.

There is relevant previous work examining the relationship between representational dimensionality and adversarial robustness (Leavitt and Morcos, Linking average- and worst-case perturbation robustness via class selectivity and dimensionality; Sanyal et al., Robustness vai Deep Low-rank Representations; Nayebi and Ganguli, Biologically inspired protection of deep networks from adversarial attacks). These papers show that low rank representations confer adversarial robustness. Can you reconcile these results with your findings that adversarial robustness is associated with greater utilization of model capacity? Additionally, most of these works (certainly Leavitt and Morcos and Sanyal et al.) regularized representational dimensionality and found that it improved robustness; it would be quite interesting to investigate whether that the inverse holds: does adversarial training reduce representational dimensionality?

The claim that “Deeper layers overfit during robust learning” seems like it could be leveraged to improve the generalization gap caused by robust learning. The simplest approach would be to simply stop training when the adversarial loss begins to rise (as in Figure 6). I assume this has drawbacks, but the authors don’t present the data (e.g. accuracy curves over training). Is this overfitting a necessary component of robust learning?

Lines 59-61: “On the other hand, the representations of benign and perturbed inputs from robust networks are indistinguishable from one another with regards to representation similarity metrics.” I think to make this claim the authors need to directly compare CKA(Benign, Perturbed) to CKA(Benign, Benign) and CKA(Perturbed, Perturbed).

The caption for Figure 2 should describe the model architecture(s) used to generate the results, as well as whether the data are benign or perturbed. There are other Figures which lack important experimental details, such as whether the data are benign or perturbed.

Comparing the CKA results in Figure 2d to the results in Figures 2a-c make it clear that the similarity between robust and non-robust networks is lower than the similarity between networks of the same type, but I think it’s important to have a negative baseline—how low is “low”? Accordingly, I think the authors should repeat the analysis (CKA between robust and non-robust networks) using sample-shuffled data (and/or some other suitable random baseline).

Showing that the block structure effect varies with the strength of adversarial training is a nice experiment and result.

The results presented in lines 174-188 (“Impact of robust training strength” and “Impact of architecture”) would be easier to interpret (and their motivation clearer) if you introduced them with Nguyen et al.’s finding that “block structure in the internal representations arises in models that are heavily overparameterized relative to the training dataset.” While you do cite their work (“Previously Nguyen et al. [28] observed that increasing network width, thus capacity, leads to emergence of block-structure in non-robust networks”), I think this is an insufficient description of their results and should be presented earlier.

“Overfitting is predominantly visible in later layers”: I would suggest a follow-up experiment to more thoroughly test this claim: freeze early layers (blocks 1 and/or 2) early in training. If early layers aren’t involved in the overfitting and training instability, then the freezing should have minimal effect on these phenomena.

Lines 278-280: “When using CKA to compare against the Snow threat model, we observe that the highest average similarity is achieved with Gabor. This represents a novel insight into these threat classes, as correspondence between the two was not previously known.” This result seems very specific and not particularly useful. I would encourage the authors to find a more general result (see my earlier comment about examining different attack types).

minor comments:

Lines 21-22: “...such as images, speech or text”. It’s a matter of personal taste, but I am a proponent of the Oxford comma: “...such as images, speech, or text”

Line 22: What does “meaningful” mean in this context? Meaningful with regards to what?

Line 187: “These results suggest that while increasing width in robust networks doesn’t lead a drastic shift in similarity of internal layer representations.” Typo?

Typo in caption of Figure 5 (...to understand similairty)
reviews.limitations: The authors devote a paragraph at the end of the discussion to the limitations of their study, which seems appropriate given the space limitations. I do, however, think the authors could be more careful and nuanced about some of their claims.
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths: The paper follows in a well-established tradition of using representational similarity analysis to understand neural network behavior and training interventions. The analyses are well-motivated and straightforward. The results are generally presented clearly and easy to understand. The results are interesting.

Weaknesses: The paper seems somewhat limited in scope: It primarily addresses adversarial (worst-case) robustness, which is only one type of robustness. I'm not totally convinced of the utility of this work; it could at the very least do a better job situating itself within existing robustness research. It's unclear if any of the experiments were run in replicate. The figures captions could be more informative and self-contained.

Overall, I think this work could be suitable for publication if it is sufficiently revised.

Questions:

The caption for Figure 2 should describe the model architecture(s) used to generate the results, as well as whether the data are benign or perturbed. There are other Figures which lack important experimental details, such as whether the data are benign or perturbed.

Comparing the CKA results in Figure 2d to the results in Figures 2a-c make it clear that the similarity between robust and non-robust networks is lower than the similarity between networks of the same type, but I think it’s important to have a negative baseline—how low is “low”? Accordingly, I think the authors should repeat the analysis (CKA between robust and non-robust networks) using sample-shuffled data (and/or some other suitable random baseline).

Showing that the block structure effect varies with the strength of adversarial training is a nice experiment and result.

The results presented in lines 174-188 (“Impact of robust training strength” and “Impact of architecture”) would be easier to interpret (and their motivation clearer) if you introduced them with Nguyen et al.’s finding that “block structure in the internal representations arises in models that are heavily overparameterized relative to the training dataset.” While you do cite their work (“Previously Nguyen et al. [28] observed that increasing network width, thus capacity, leads to emergence of block-structure in non-robust networks”), I think this is an insufficient description of their results and should be presented earlier.

“Overfitting is predominantly visible in later layers”: I would suggest a follow-up experiment to more thoroughly test this claim: freeze early layers (blocks 1 and/or 2) early in training. If early layers aren’t involved in the overfitting and training instability, then the freezing should have minimal effect on these phenomena.

Lines 278-280: “When using CKA to compare against the Snow threat model, we observe that the highest average similarity is achieved with Gabor. This represents a novel insight into these threat classes, as correspondence between the two was not previously known.” This result seems very specific and not particularly useful. I would encourage the authors to find a more general result (see my earlier comment about examining different attack types).

minor comments:

Lines 21-22: “...such as images, speech or text”. It’s a matter of personal taste, but I am a proponent of the Oxford comma: “...such as images, speech, or text”

Line 22: What does “meaningful” mean in this context? Meaningful with regards to what?

Line 187: “These results suggest that while increasing width in robust networks doesn’t lead a drastic shift in similarity of internal layer representations.” Typo?

Typo in caption of Figure 5 (...to understand similairty)

Limitations:
The authors devote a paragraph at the end of the discussion to the limitations of their study, which seems appropriate given the space limitations. I do, however, think the authors could be more careful and nuanced about some of their claims.

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Summary

This paper investigates how adversarial robustness training reshapes neural network representations. The authors employ CKA (Centered Kernel Alignment) as their primary analytical tool to compare representations across robust and standard networks, benign and adversarial inputs, and different stages of training. Their key findings include:

- Robust networks develop less specialized representations; similarity between distant layers increases, and the characteristic block structure weakens
- Early-layer representations in robust networks exhibit minimal sensitivity to adversarial perturbations; benign and perturbed inputs produce comparable representations
- Later layers exhibit signs of overfitting during robust training
- Networks trained against different adversarial threat models converge on similar representational structures

## Strengths and Weaknesses

**Strengths:** This work appropriately builds upon the established literature using representational similarity analysis to characterize neural network behavior and the effects of training procedures. The research questions are well-motivated and the analytical approach is transparent. The presentation of results is generally accessible and intuitive. The substantive findings contribute meaningfully to our understanding of robust learning.

**Weaknesses:** The scope appears somewhat narrow, focusing exclusively on adversarial (worst-case) robustness while neglecting other robustness paradigms. The paper would benefit from better contextualizing its contributions within the broader robustness literature and clarifying the practical significance of the findings. It remains unclear whether experiments were conducted with multiple random seeds or initializations. Figure captions require enhancement to be more self-explanatory and comprehensive in documenting experimental details.

The work appears suitable for publication contingent upon substantial revisions addressing the concerns outlined below.

## Questions and Suggestions

The analysis concentrates on adversarial robustness in the worst-case sense, which represents just one facet of a broader robustness landscape encompassing average-case perturbations (as documented in Hendrycks and Dietterich's work on common corruptions) and naturally occurring adversarial examples (Hendrycks et al.). The foundational importance of adversarial robustness itself remains contested within the research community (Gilmer et al.'s work raises important questions about the framing of adversarial research). While the authors do examine Gabor, snow, and jpeg attacks, this treatment remains somewhat superficial. I would recommend a more comprehensive investigation of how the observed phenomena generalize across different robustness types.

A critical methodological concern: Do the reported results derive from single model instances? If so, I would urge the authors to conduct at minimum three independent runs with different random seeds. Should computational constraints prohibit this, the focus should narrow to the most impactful findings.

Important prior work has explored connections between representational dimensionality and adversarial robustness (Leavitt and Morcos demonstrate that low-rank representations enhance robustness; Sanyal et al. show similar benefits through deep low-rank approaches; Nayebi and Ganguli present biologically-inspired protective mechanisms). These studies collectively suggest that reduced dimensionality supports robustness. This appears to tension with your characterization of robust networks as utilizing greater model capacity. How do these observations reconcile? Furthermore, since prior work achieved robustness improvements through dimensionality regularization, would it be instructive to investigate whether adversarial training itself produces dimensionality reduction?

Your observation regarding "overfitting in deeper layers during robust learning" presents an actionable opportunity for improving the generalization performance gap. The most straightforward intervention would involve early stopping when adversarial loss begins degrading (as suggested by Figure 6). While this likely has limitations, these are not discussed. Can you provide training curves demonstrating loss trajectories? Is this layer-wise overfitting an inevitable consequence of robust training?

**Line 59-61 concern:** The claim that "representations of benign and perturbed inputs from robust networks are indistinguishable from one another with regards to representation similarity metrics" requires more rigorous support. Direct quantitative comparison between CKA(Benign, Perturbed), CKA(Benign, Benign), and CKA(Perturbed, Perturbed) would substantiate this claim more convincingly.

**Figure 2 caption:** The caption requires explicit specification of the model architecture(s), as well as clarification regarding whether inputs are benign or adversarially perturbed. Multiple figures throughout the manuscript omit such crucial experimental details.

**Establishing meaningful baselines:** While Figure 2d clearly demonstrates that robust and non-robust network similarity falls below within-type similarity, the absolute magnitude remains ambiguous. What constitutes genuinely "low" similarity? The analysis would strengthen considerably if you repeated the robust-versus-non-robust comparison using shuffled data or other appropriate random controls to establish meaningful baselines.

The analysis of how block structure intensity correlates with adversarial training strength represents a valuable experimental contribution with clear interpretive value.

**Lines 174-188 ("Impact of robust training strength" and "Impact of architecture"):** This section would benefit from earlier introduction of Nguyen et al.'s observation that "block structure emerges in heavily overparameterized models relative to dataset size." While you do reference this work, the description ("Previously Nguyen et al. [28] observed that increasing network width, thus capacity, leads to emergence of block-structure in non-robust networks") undersells their contribution. Positioning this framework earlier would enhance clarity and motivation.

**Testing the early-layer hypothesis:** The claim that "overfitting concentrates in later layers" deserves empirical validation through an additional experiment: freeze early layers (blocks 1 and/or 2) at various training stages. If early layers truly remain uninvolved in overfitting and instability, freezing should produce minimal disruption to these phenomena.

**Lines 278-280 finding:** The observation that Gabor attacks show highest similarity with Snow perturbations appears highly specialized and of limited general interest. I would encourage identification of more broadly applicable insights regarding the relationships among different threat models.

**Minor Issues:**

- Lines 21-22: Consider adopting the Oxford comma: "...such as images, speech, or text"
- Line 22: Define "meaningful" more precisely within your specific context
- Line 187: Appears to contain a grammatical error: "...doesn't lead a drastic shift..." 
- Figure 5 caption: Spelling error ("similairty")

## Limitations

The authors appropriately acknowledge study limitations in their discussion, which seems reasonable given space constraints. However, some claims would benefit from more careful qualification and nuanced framing.

## Recommendation

**Rating: 7 - Accept**

This is a technically sound paper making meaningful contributions to understanding representational changes under adversarial robustness training. The work demonstrates good-to-excellent experimental design and evaluation, with solid reproducibility practices. While the scope is somewhat circumscribed and several methodological refinements would strengthen the contribution, the findings have notable impact within the adversarial robustness subarea and moderate relevance across the broader robustness literature. The paper merits publication following incorporation of the substantive revisions outlined above.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The authors examine the effects of adversarial robustness training on representation. Specifically, they use CKA to compare robust vs non-robust networks, benign vs. adversarial inputs, and how these comparisons change over the course of learning. They find the following results:
- Robust representations are less specialized; distant layers are more similar in robust networks, and block structure is weaker
- Early layers in robust networks are largely unaffected by adversarial examples; representations are similar for benign vs. perturbed inputs
- Deeper layers overfit during robust learning
- Models trained to be robust to different threat models have similar representations

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths: The paper follows in a well-established tradition of using representational similarity analysis to understand neural network behavior and training interventions. The analyses are well-motivated and straightforward. The results are generally presented clearly and easy to understand. The results are interesting.

Weaknesses: The paper seems somewhat limited in scope: It primarily addresses adversarial (worst-case) robustness, which is only one type of robustness. I'm not totally convinced of the utility of this work; it could at the very least do a better job situating itself within existing robustness research. It's unclear if any of the experiments were run in replicate. The figures captions could be more informative and self-contained.

Overall, I think this work could be suitable for publication if it is sufficiently revised.

Questions:
This work primarily examines adversarial (i.e. worst-case) robustness, which is one of many types of robustness, among others including average-case robustness (Hendrycks and Dietterich, Benchmarking Neural Network Robustness to Common Corruptions and Perturbations) and natural adversarial examples (Hendrycks et al.). It’s not clear how important of a problem adversarial robustness is (see Gilmer et al.’s Motivating the Rules of the Game for Adversarial Example Research). The authors examine robustness to Gabor, snow, and jpeg attacks, but the analysis and results are limited. I would encourage the researchers to more systematically examine how their findings extend to other types of robustness.

Are the results from a single instance of each model? If so, I would strongly encourage the authors to repeat their analyses in triplicate at a minimum. If this is computationally cost-prohibitive, then perhaps they could focus on the most important results.

There is relevant previous work examining the relationship between representational dimensionality and adversarial robustness (Leavitt and Morcos, Linking average- and worst-case perturbation robustness via class selectivity and dimensionality; Sanyal et al., Robustness vai Deep Low-rank Representations; Nayebi and Ganguli, Biologically inspired protection of deep networks from adversarial attacks). These papers show that low rank representations confer adversarial robustness. Can you reconcile these results with your findings that adversarial robustness is associated with greater utilization of model capacity? Additionally, most of these works (certainly Leavitt and Morcos and Sanyal et al.) regularized representational dimensionality and found that it improved robustness; it would be quite interesting to investigate whether that the inverse holds: does adversarial training reduce representational dimensionality?

The claim that “Deeper layers overfit during robust learning” seems like it could be leveraged to improve the generalization gap caused by robust learning. The simplest approach would be to simply stop training when the adversarial loss begins to rise (as in Figure 6). I assume this has drawbacks, but the authors don’t present the data (e.g. accuracy curves over training). Is this overfitting a necessary component of robust learning?

Lines 59-61: “On the other hand, the representations of benign and perturbed inputs from robust networks are indistinguishable from one another with regards to representation similarity metrics.” I think to make this claim the authors need to directly compare CKA(Benign, Perturbed) to CKA(Benign, Benign) and CKA(Perturbed, Perturbed).

The caption for Figure 2 should describe the model architecture(s) used to generate the results, as well as whether the data are benign or perturbed. There are other Figures which lack important experimental details, such as whether the data are benign or perturbed.

Comparing the CKA results in Figure 2d to the results in Figures 2a-c make it clear that the similarity between robust and non-robust networks is lower than the similarity between networks of the same type, but I think it’s important to have a negative baseline—how low is “low”? Accordingly, I think the authors should repeat the analysis (CKA between robust and non-robust networks) using sample-shuffled data (and/or some other suitable random baseline).

Showing that the block structure effect varies with the strength of adversarial training is a nice experiment and result.

The results presented in lines 174-188 (“Impact of robust training strength” and “Impact of architecture”) would be easier to interpret (and their motivation clearer) if you introduced them with Nguyen et al.’s finding that “block structure in the internal representations arises in models that are heavily overparameterized relative to the training dataset.” While you do cite their work (“Previously Nguyen et al. [28] observed that increasing network width, thus capacity, leads to emergence of block-structure in non-robust networks”), I think this is an insufficient description of their results and should be presented earlier.

“Overfitting is predominantly visible in later layers”: I would suggest a follow-up experiment to more thoroughly test this claim: freeze early layers (blocks 1 and/or 2) early in training. If early layers aren’t involved in the overfitting and training instability, then the freezing should have minimal effect on these phenomena.

Lines 278-280: “When using CKA to compare against the Snow threat model, we observe that the highest average similarity is achieved with Gabor. This represents a novel insight into these threat classes, as correspondence between the two was not previously known.” This result seems very specific and not particularly useful. I would encourage the authors to find a more general result (see my earlier comment about examining different attack types).

minor comments:

Lines 21-22: “...such as images, speech or text”. It’s a matter of personal taste, but I am a proponent of the Oxford comma: “...such as images, speech, or text”

Line 22: What does “meaningful” mean in this context? Meaningful with regards to what?

Line 187: “These results suggest that while increasing width in robust networks doesn’t lead a drastic shift in similarity of internal layer representations.” Typo?

Typo in caption of Figure 5 (...to understand similairty)

Limitations:
The authors devote a paragraph at the end of the discussion to the limitations of their study, which seems appropriate given the space limitations. I do, however, think the authors could be more careful and nuanced about some of their claims.

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This paper uses an existing method for comparisons of intermediate neural network activations (CKA) for a comparison of robust and non-robust networks. The authors analyze the similarities in different aspects and try to deduce insights in adversarial training.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
- The paper uses an interesting & potentially insightful approach to better understand how adversarially trained networks process information and how they differ from non-robust networks.
- The attached code for this submission is well documented and looks clean, making the results more trustworthy.
- It feels like the paper spends too much time describing plots/results compared to interpreting the results and presenting hypotheses for what results mean.
reviews.questions:
- Missing related work: There has been previous work on comparing the features of robust vs. non-robust neural networks that should be cited, e.g. using feature visualizations [1].
- L25: Reference missing.
- L45f: Reference [18] should be placed behind Imagewoof and not behind Imagenet; also, a reference for ImageNet is missing. 
- L93: E.g., JPEG compression doesn't seem like an additive perturbation.
- L108: If this is well known, I encourage the authors to support this sentence with (multiple) references.
- L118: A more detailed critical discussion of the possible shortcomings of these metrics and why they don't impact the results of this paper would be good.
- L123f: Why doesn't this impact your results? This definitely needs to be supported by a strong argument. 
- Figure 2/3: On which datasets were these metrics calculated? On the vanilla test data or on adversarially perturbed versions of the test data? What does the figure look like for the other data type (either benign or adversarial)?
- Figure 2/3: Given that the clean accuracy goes down for adversarial training, I'm wondering whether the change in the similarity plots is really due to a special aspect of adversarial training or just due to the drop in performance, i.e. how do these plots look like for non-robust networks that are not trained until convergence but until they reach a similar test accuracy as the robust networks?
- L163f: That seems a bit like an overstatement: The block structure is still clearly visible for 2 out of the 3 datasets - it just gets weaker.
- Multiple typos/grammar mistakes that make it sometimes break the flow of the text, e.g. L172, L187, L201, L216, L221, L235.
- Figure 4: These plots should be larger - the text is hard to read.
- Figure 4: The first sentence of the caption doesn't read right/is difficult to parse.
- L185f: Did the authors observe the same behavior as described by Nguyen et al. when they tried to reproduce their observations for non-robust networks? At the moment it is difficult to confidently say that the observation reported here is because of the robust network or because of the experimental setup used by the authors.
- L213: If this is well known, I again encourage the authors to support this sentence with (multiple) references. Furthermore, this is actually not such a clear property, and there are specific attacks that just aim to create adversarial examples that transfer well between different models.
- Minor comment: The paper uses inconsistent notation - sometimes the authors say "Figure", sometimes "figure"; sometimes "Appendix", sometimes "App.".
- L252f: Where can we see both validation and training loss? The figure only shows one "loss" but doesn't say which one it is.
- L265: I wouldn't call these "adversarial perturbations" but rather use the more commonly used expression common corruptions. Especially, since at least for JPEG compression there is nothing you can optimize - so that doesn't really fit the overall adversarial framework.
- Section 6: What are the specific parameters of the JPEG, snow, and Gabor corruptions?
- L289: What is the conclusion/interpretation of the results?

[1] Leveraging Sparse Linear Layers for Debuggable Deep Networks. Eric Wong, Shibani Santurkar, Aleksander Mądry. 2021
reviews.limitations:
- While the authors mentioned criticism on the similarity metric they use (CKA), they don't really explain why this doesn't apply to their analysis. This should be properly addressed.
- It is also unclear whether the results differ if a different adversarial attack (with the same thread model) was used for generating the adversarial perturbations - this should ideally also be addressed.

Summary:
This paper uses an existing method for comparisons of intermediate neural network activations (CKA) for a comparison of robust and non-robust networks. The authors analyze the similarities in different aspects and try to deduce insights in adversarial training.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
- The paper uses an interesting & potentially insightful approach to better understand how adversarially trained networks process information and how they differ from non-robust networks.
- The attached code for this submission is well documented and looks clean, making the results more trustworthy.
- It feels like the paper spends too much time describing plots/results compared to interpreting the results and presenting hypotheses for what results mean.

Questions:
- Missing related work: There has been previous work on comparing the features of robust vs. non-robust neural networks that should be cited, e.g. using feature visualizations [1].
- L25: Reference missing.
- L45f: Reference [18] should be placed behind Imagewoof and not behind Imagenet; also, a reference for ImageNet is missing. 
- L93: E.g., JPEG compression doesn't seem like an additive perturbation.
- L108: If this is well known, I encourage the authors to support this sentence with (multiple) references.
- L118: A more detailed critical discussion of the possible shortcomings of these metrics and why they don't impact the results of this paper would be good.
- L123f: Why doesn't this impact your results? This definitely needs to be supported by a strong argument. 
- Figure 2/3: On which datasets were these metrics calculated? On the vanilla test data or on adversarially perturbed versions of the test data? What does the figure look like for the other data type (either benign or adversarial)?
- Figure 2/3: Given that the clean accuracy goes down for adversarial training, I'm wondering whether the change in the similarity plots is really due to a special aspect of adversarial training or just due to the drop in performance, i.e. how do these plots look like for non-robust networks that are not trained until convergence but until they reach a similar test accuracy as the robust networks?
- L163f: That seems a bit like an overstatement: The block structure is still clearly visible for 2 out of the 3 datasets - it just gets weaker.
- Multiple typos/grammar mistakes that make it sometimes break the flow of the text, e.g. L172, L187, L201, L216, L221, L235.
- Figure 4: These plots should be larger - the text is hard to read.
- Figure 4: The first sentence of the caption doesn't read right/is difficult to parse.
- L185f: Did the authors observe the same behavior as described by Nguyen et al. when they tried to reproduce their observations for non-robust networks? At the moment it is difficult to confidently say that the observation reported here is because of the robust network or because of the experimental setup used by the authors.
- L213: If this is well known, I again encourage the authors to support this sentence with (multiple) references. Furthermore, this is actually not such a clear property, and there are specific attacks that just aim to create adversarial examples that transfer well between different models.
- Minor comment: The paper uses inconsistent notation - sometimes the authors say "Figure", sometimes "figure"; sometimes "Appendix", sometimes "App.".
- L252f: Where can we see both validation and training loss? The figure only shows one "loss" but doesn't say which one it is.
- L265: I wouldn't call these "adversarial perturbations" but rather use the more commonly used expression common corruptions. Especially, since at least for JPEG compression there is nothing you can optimize - so that doesn't really fit the overall adversarial framework.
- Section 6: What are the specific parameters of the JPEG, snow, and Gabor corruptions?
- L289: What is the conclusion/interpretation of the results?

[1] Leveraging Sparse Linear Layers for Debuggable Deep Networks. Eric Wong, Shibani Santurkar, Aleksander Mądry. 2021

Limitations:

- It is also unclear whether the results differ if a different adversarial attack (with the same thread model) was used for generating the adversarial perturbations - this should ideally also be addressed.

reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

This work applies Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA), an established technique for analyzing intermediate layer activations, to investigate the representational differences between adversarially trained and standard neural networks. The authors leverage this approach to extract insights about how adversarial training shapes network information processing.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

**Strengths:**
- The paper adopts a compelling and potentially valuable methodology to elucidate the mechanisms by which adversarially trained networks encode information and how these mechanisms diverge from those in non-robust models.
- The accompanying code implementation is thorough, well-structured, and transparent, which enhances confidence in the reported findings.

**Weaknesses:**
- The manuscript allocates disproportionate space to describing and documenting empirical observations relative to synthesizing these findings into meaningful interpretations and theoretical propositions about their significance.

## Detailed Comments and Questions

**Literature and References:**
- The paper overlooks relevant prior work examining feature differences between robust and non-robust networks, particularly studies employing feature visualization techniques [1], which should be incorporated.
- Line 25 contains an incomplete reference.
- Lines 45-46: Reference [18] should be associated with Imagewoof rather than ImageNet; additionally, ImageNet itself requires a citation.
- Line 108: If this claim represents established knowledge, the statement should be substantiated with appropriate citations.
- Line 213: Similarly, this assertion would benefit from supporting references. Moreover, the claim itself is not unambiguous, as certain attack methodologies specifically target transferability across models.

**Technical and Methodological Concerns:**
- Line 93: The characterization of JPEG compression as an additive perturbation appears problematic.
- Line 118: A more thorough and critical examination of potential limitations inherent to these metrics, and an explanation of why such limitations do not compromise the paper's conclusions, would strengthen the work.
- Lines 123-124: The claim that certain metric limitations do not affect the results requires robust justification and argumentation.
- Figures 2-3: Clarification is needed regarding which data variants were used for metric computation—clean test data, adversarially perturbed test data, or both? Additionally, comparative visualizations for both conditions would be informative.
- Figures 2-3: Given that adversarial training incurs a clean accuracy penalty, it remains unclear whether the observed changes in similarity patterns reflect genuine characteristics of adversarial training or merely reflect reduced model performance. Comparisons with standard networks trained to equivalent accuracy levels would help disambiguate this.
- Lines 163-164: The statement appears overstated; block structure remains visibly present in two of three datasets, albeit diminished.
- Lines 185-186: Did the authors successfully replicate Nguyen et al.'s findings when applying their methodology to non-robust networks? Without this validation, it is difficult to attribute the reported observations definitively to adversarial robustness rather than to experimental design choices.
- Line 265: The terminology "adversarial perturbations" is imprecise here; "common corruptions" would be more accurate and conventional. JPEG compression in particular is not amenable to optimization, making it incongruent with the adversarial framework.
- Section 6: The specific parameter values for JPEG, snow, and Gabor corruptions are not specified.
- Line 289: The section lacks a clear statement of conclusions or interpretation of the presented results.
- Line 252-253: The figure caption references both validation and training loss, yet the figure itself shows only a single loss curve without specifying which variant is displayed.

**Presentation Issues:**
- Figure 4: The figures are too small; text is difficult to read and should be enlarged.
- Figure 4: The opening sentence of the caption is awkwardly constructed and difficult to parse.
- Notation inconsistency: The manuscript alternates between "Figure" and "figure," and between "Appendix" and "App."
- Multiple typographical and grammatical errors appear throughout (e.g., lines 172, 187, 201, 216, 221, 235), occasionally disrupting readability.

## Limitations Section

**Unresolved Issues:**
- While the authors acknowledge criticisms of CKA as a similarity metric, they do not adequately explain why these criticisms do not undermine their analysis. This concern warrants more thorough treatment.
- The generalizability of findings across different adversarial attacks (within the same threat model) remains unexplored and should be investigated, as results may be sensitive to attack choice.

## Recommendation

**Rating: 6 - Weak Accept**

This paper presents technically sound work with moderate-to-high potential impact. The evaluation methodology is appropriate, reproducibility concerns are minimal, and no significant issues arise regarding resources or ethical considerations. However, the work would benefit from more rigorous interpretation of results, clarification of methodological choices, and more comprehensive treatment of limitations before publication.

reviews.summary: The paper contrasts representation similarities of networks trained to perform image classification with and without adversarial noise. To do so, the authors measure the similarity of representations using the Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) metric (as well two other similarity metrics in appendix) for CIFAR-10 and two subsets of ImageNet. The authors highlight 1) networks trained with adversarial noise have layers similar to one another compared to those of standard trained networks (which have a block structure) 2) representations in early layers are unaffected by adversarial perturbations both for standard and adversarially trained networks 3) networks trained with and without adversarial perturbations have similar representations until the last 10 or so layers 4) early layers converge faster and later layers overfit to local minima. The authors also analyze the similarity of representations when other perturbations are applied JPEG, Gabor, and Snow, finding
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
This work investigates representations learned for image classification by contrasting the representation similarities of networks trained with and without adversarial noise—leading to several insights into properties of the learned representations as well as learning dynamics of modern networks. Such properties as the authors point out are not captured by aggregate performance metrics such as loss or accuracy, leading to insights about the learned representations. The paper is well-written, experiments clearly described, and motivation is well-grounded.

The experiments conducted are convincing and well-formulated. For example, multiple similarity metrics are compared, variants of the adversarial perturbations are explored, and experimental claims are well-founded. 
However, the authors'claims should be sufficiently couched within the experimental settings studied: supervised image classification for ResNet-based models on CIFAR-10 and two subsets of ImageNet. For example, the work only studies ResNet-based architectures yet claims to cover “DNNs with different architectures” (line 12) and the “impact of choice of architecture” (line 37). I would expect a comparison of “different architectures” to encompass for example transformer-based architectures, MLP-architectures, etc. I suggest the authors more explicitly couch claims in the introduction, abstract, title, and conclusions within the confines of the experimental settings studied. For example 
The comparison of representations for "threat models" (JPEG, Gabor, and Snow) in Section 6 was not particularly informative and seemed removed from the primary findings of the remainder of the paper. 

Robust representations is a broad term. This work studies robustness to random noise in the input. Yet, the authors intermix “robustness to adversarial examples” and often plainly use the term “robust network” to describe a particular type: robustness to noise (defined via L-p bounds on the input) throughout the work. For example, robust in the context of image classification can just as well refer to robustness with respect to rendering method ImageNet-Sketch, artifacts such as blurring (ImageNet-C), adversarial examples (ImageNet-A), or even robustness to natural transformations such as pose (Alcorn et al.). I suggest the authors clarify the wording to only include the specific definitions of robustness studied here.

The insights gained from the authors’ analysis are interesting and well-described. The finding of most value, in my opinion, is that later layers overfit (matching existing work relating overfitting in later layers to spurious correlations [1]). While the analysis sheds light on differences between networks trained with and without adversarial noise as well as their learning dynamics the findings are confined to the L-infinity definition of robustness studied for supervised image classification using ResNet-based architectures (for the main claims in section 4 and 5).

[1] “Last Layer Re-Training is Sufficient for Robustness to Spurious Correlations” [https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.02937](https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.02937)
reviews.questions: included above
reviews.limitations: Yes.
