reviews.summary: The paper introduces a data augmentation technique for behavioral cloning algorithms. It consists of adding Gaussian noise to the states used for training and storing the perturbed states and the action the expert policy would produce in such states. Using this augmented dataset, the proposed algorithm achieves better performance than the baselines over three complex continuous tasks.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper is well written and contains many implementation details and ablation studies.

The idea proposed is very simple and effective. Unfortunately, apart from the experimental results in complex environments, the novelty in this work is limited. It is well known that augmenting the dataset in Behavioral Cloning is useful and there are prior works applying observation perturbation and label corrections via experts queries [1,2,3].

The main motivation for using the proposed approach is the case when collecting more data in the environment is expensive, but querying the expert many times is not. The authors motivate this by saying that "an expert policy may be too large to execute due to memory considerations" and that "Many expert-driven learning approaches actually have access to an expert that can be queried", but no specific example is provided to the reader. 

The comparison with baselines BC and ABC does not seem very fair. If the policy has enough capacity and the distribution from which we sample the states covers the state-space enough, one could clone the expert by repeatedly querying it on such random states without even seeing a single trajectory. Therefore it is quite straightforward that the proposed method works better than the baselines.  

[1] M. Bojarski et al., “End to end learning for self-driving cars,” arXiv preprint, 2016

Summary:
The paper introduces a data augmentation technique for behavioral cloning algorithms. It consists of adding Gaussian noise to the states used for training and storing the perturbed states and the action the expert policy would produce in such states. Using this augmented dataset, the proposed algorithm achieves better performance than the baselines over three complex continuous tasks.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
The paper is well written and contains many implementation details and ablation studies.

The idea proposed is very simple and effective. Unfortunately, apart from the experimental results in complex environments, the novelty in this work is limited. It is well known that augmenting the dataset in Behavioral Cloning is useful and there are prior works applying observation perturbation and label corrections via experts queries [1,2,3].

The main motivation for using the proposed approach is the case when collecting more data in the environment is expensive, but querying the expert many times is not. The authors motivate this by saying that "an expert policy may be too large to execute due to memory considerations" and that "Many expert-driven learning approaches actually have access to an expert that can be queried", but no specific example is provided to the reader. 

The comparison with baselines BC and ABC does not seem very fair. If the policy has enough capacity and the distribution from which we sample the states covers the state-space enough, one could clone the expert by repeatedly querying it on such random states without even seeing a single trajectory. Therefore it is quite straightforward that the proposed method works better than the baselines.

[3] T. Buhet, E. Wirbel, and X. Perrotton, “Conditional Vehicle Trajectories Prediction in CARLA Urban Environment,” in ICCV, 2019

reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Summary

This work proposes a data augmentation strategy for behavioral cloning that involves perturbing training states with Gaussian noise and obtaining corresponding action labels from an expert policy queried on these perturbed states. The authors demonstrate that training on this augmented dataset yields improved results compared to standard behavioral cloning baselines across three challenging continuous control problems.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

The manuscript is clearly presented with comprehensive implementation details and thorough ablation studies that support the main findings.

The core contribution—augmenting behavioral cloning data through state perturbation and expert relabeling—is straightforward yet demonstrates practical effectiveness. However, the conceptual novelty is somewhat limited given existing literature on data augmentation for imitation learning. Prior work has already explored observation noise injection and leveraging expert feedback to correct action labels [1,2,3], which substantially overlaps with the proposed approach.

The authors position their method as valuable when environment interactions are costly but expert queries are inexpensive. While they offer some justification—such as memory constraints on expert policies and the general availability of queryable experts—these arguments lack concrete instantiation or real-world examples that would strengthen the motivation.

The baseline comparisons raise fairness concerns. Given sufficient model capacity and adequate coverage of the state space through random sampling, one could effectively learn the expert policy by simply querying it repeatedly on diverse states without requiring any trajectory data. This makes the superiority of the proposed method over basic behavioral cloning and ABC somewhat expected and less surprising.

[1] M. Bojarski et al., "End to end learning for self-driving cars," arXiv preprint, 2016

[2] L. George, T. Buhet, E. Wirbel, G. Le-Gall, and X. Perrotton, "Imitation learning for end to end vehicle longitudinal control with forward camera," in NeurIPS, 2018.

[3] T. Buhet, E. Wirbel, and X. Perrotton, "Conditional Vehicle Trajectories Prediction in CARLA Urban Environment," in ICCV, 2019

## Questions and Suggestions

In continuous control settings, Gaussian perturbations typically result in physically plausible states that an agent could feasibly encounter. This property does not necessarily hold for visual observations. Could the authors provide intuition or empirical evidence explaining why this augmentation strategy would be beneficial when working with image-based state representations?

Consider the scenario where the expert's training distribution is characterized by mean μ and standard deviation σ (estimated from recent expert trajectories). How would the method perform if initialized with zero trajectories and trained exclusively on synthetically generated data sampled from a normal distribution with these estimated parameters?

Figures 1 and Algorithm 1 appear substantially before their first reference in the text. The presentation would benefit from relocating these elements to coincide with their initial mention in the manuscript.

## Limitations Discussion

The authors acknowledge that their approach requires frequent expert queries, which could become problematic if such queries incur significant cost. I recommend providing concrete scenarios or application domains where expert queries are prohibitively expensive, to better contextualize this limitation and clarify when the method is and is not applicable.

## Overall Assessment

The work presents technically sound methodology with solid experimental validation. No major concerns arise regarding evaluation rigor, resource requirements, or reproducibility. While the novelty is moderate and the motivation could be strengthened with specific examples, the practical effectiveness and comprehensive experimental treatment support acceptance.

**Rating: 6 - Weak Accept**: Technically solid work with moderate-to-high impact and no major concerns regarding evaluation, resources, reproducibility, or ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a simple yet effective way APC, which queries the parametric experts to augment the demonstrations for imitation learning. APC is shown to outperform naive behavior cloning in both expert compression and transferring from privileged expert settings.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Pros:
This paper focuses on a novel problem where a parametric expert can be queried. The paper writing is clear and easy to follow. The empirical results do demonstrate the advantage of APC over BC and naive ABC. 

Cons:
However, I don't feel this paper proposes any novel idea. One serious issue is that when compared with baselines, all these baselines don't query the expert as APC does. This means that the baselines have fewer demonstrations to imitate from. It is clear that APC will beat naive ABC since APC has more high-quality/ground-truth demonstrations. Therefore, I think this doesn't make enough technical contributions to the problem it studies.
reviews.questions:
I think the key problem here is that the authors don't explore the methods of querying experts enough. From my point of view, the advantage of APC is totally brought by a new setting where the expert could be queried, which is not an innovation of the methods. I suggest the authors compare APC with more baselines that can query the experts. 

Although this method is reasonable, I'm afraid the current technical contribution is far from enough. Adding the Gaussian noise is the simplest way to make a data augmentation, and previous works (e.g., RAD, https://arxiv.org/pdf/2004.14990.pdf) also have systematically studied the data augmentations for general reinforcement learning problems. It could be better if the authors come up with novel methods for data augmentations in your settings.
reviews.limitations: The authors have addressed the limitations.

reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

The paper introduces APC, a methodology that leverages queries to parametric experts in order to enhance the quality and quantity of demonstration data for imitation learning tasks. The approach demonstrates improvements relative to standard behavior cloning, particularly in scenarios involving expert model compression and transfer learning from settings with privileged information access.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

**Strengths:**
The work tackles an interesting and relatively underexplored problem formulation centered on interactive querying of parametric experts. The presentation is well-structured and accessible, making it straightforward for readers to follow the main contributions. The experimental evaluation successfully shows that APC achieves superior performance compared to both standard BC and naive ABC approaches.

**Weaknesses:**
A fundamental concern is that the paper lacks sufficient methodological novelty. The comparison setup raises a critical fairness issue: the baseline methods do not employ expert querying, meaning they operate with a smaller and less curated set of demonstrations. This creates an uneven playing field where APC's performance gains appear to stem largely from having access to additional high-quality, ground-truth labeled examples rather than from algorithmic innovation. Given this advantage, it is unsurprising that APC outperforms naive ABC, yet this does not constitute a meaningful technical contribution.

## Questions and Suggestions

The core issue revolves around the limited exploration of expert querying strategies. The apparent advantage of APC derives primarily from introducing a new problem setting—the ability to query an expert—rather than from developing novel algorithmic techniques. To address this concern, I recommend the authors include comparisons against alternative baselines that similarly have access to expert queries, allowing for a more informative evaluation of the actual methodological contributions.

While the proposed approach is reasonable and sound, the technical novelty appears insufficient for acceptance at a top venue. The data augmentation strategy employed—adding Gaussian noise—represents the most straightforward augmentation technique available. Notably, prior work such as RAD (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2004.14990.pdf) has already conducted comprehensive investigations into data augmentation approaches within broader reinforcement learning contexts. The paper would be substantially strengthened by the introduction of more sophisticated or problem-specific augmentation methods tailored to the particular querying setting presented here.

## Limitations

The authors have adequately addressed the limitations of their work.

## Rating

**5: Borderline accept** - This is a technically competent paper where the arguments for acceptance marginally outweigh those for rejection, though with notable limitations in scope and evaluation. The work merits publication with reservations.

Cons:
However, I don't feel this paper proposes any novel idea. One serious issue is that when compared with baselines, all these baselines don't query the expert as APC does. This means that the baselines have fewer demonstrations to imitate from. It is clear that APC will beat naive ABC since APC has more high-quality/ground-truth demonstrations. Therefore, I think this doesn't make enough technical contributions to the problem it studies.

Questions:
I think the key problem here is that the authors don't explore the methods of querying experts enough. From my point of view, the advantage of APC is totally brought by a new setting where the expert could be queried, which is not an innovation of the methods. I suggest the authors compare APC with more baselines that can query the experts. 

Although this method is reasonable, I'm afraid the current technical contribution is far from enough. Adding the Gaussian noise is the simplest way to make a data augmentation, and previous works (e.g., RAD, https://arxiv.org/pdf/2004.14990.pdf) also have systematically studied the data augmentations for general reinforcement learning problems. It could be better if the authors come up with novel methods for data augmentations in your settings.

Limitations:
The authors have addressed the limitations.

title: Unsupervised Representation Learning from Pre-trained Diffusion Probabilistic Models

reviews.summary: Whereas prior work (DiffAE) uses a conditional DPM as the decoder in an auto-encoder setup, this work attempts to leverage pretrained unconditional DPMs instead. The proposed formulation extends the classifier-guidance solution for latent autoencoder semantic codes z. However, instead of explicitly training classifiers p(z | x_t), taking its corresponding score, they propose to directly fit to the posterior mean gap of the pretrained, unsupervised, and frozen DPM. Results demonstrate higher quality reconstruction than DiffAE, with an overall simpler training setup.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
strengths
- paper was clearly written. Formulation was easy to follow
- prior works using classifier guidance had some issues regarding the scale of the guidance vector being used, but I believe the proposed formulation gets around that issue by fitting to the posterior mean gap instead.

weaknesses
- In section 3.4, it's not clear how the start and end of the critical stage are determined.
- L124 Except the latent code z (awkward phrasing)
- One thing that would've been nice to see is whether it's truly necessary to use another DPM for modeling the generative distribution $z$. DiffAE justifies their choice of a DPM by stating that a VAE's objective would have been difficult to tune, but perhaps a simple auto-encoder with a post-fit GMM over the latent vectors might suffice?
reviews.questions:
- See weaknesses
- How does the closeness between the pretrained DPM's domain and the target domain affect the efficacy of this method? If the pretrained DPM were from a completely unrelated domain, would this just mean that the posterior gap is larger and that the training time of this method would take much longer?
reviews.limitations: Limitations were not discussed. Example topics would include deep fakes etc.

Summary:
Whereas prior work (DiffAE) uses a conditional DPM as the decoder in an auto-encoder setup, this work attempts to leverage pretrained unconditional DPMs instead. The proposed formulation extends the classifier-guidance solution for latent autoencoder semantic codes z. However, instead of explicitly training classifiers p(z | x_t), taking its corresponding score, they propose to directly fit to the posterior mean gap of the pretrained, unsupervised, and frozen DPM. Results demonstrate higher quality reconstruction than DiffAE, with an overall simpler training setup.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
strengths
- paper was clearly written. Formulation was easy to follow
- prior works using classifier guidance had some issues regarding the scale of the guidance vector being used, but I believe the proposed formulation gets around that issue by fitting to the posterior mean gap instead.

weaknesses
- In section 3.4, it's not clear how the start and end of the critical stage are determined.
- L124 Except the latent code z (awkward phrasing)
- One thing that would've been nice to see is whether it's truly necessary to use another DPM for modeling the generative distribution $z$. DiffAE justifies their choice of a DPM by stating that a VAE's objective would have been difficult to tune, but perhaps a simple auto-encoder with a post-fit GMM over the latent vectors might suffice?

Questions:
- See weaknesses
- How does the closeness between the pretrained DPM's domain and the target domain affect the efficacy of this method? If the pretrained DPM were from a completely unrelated domain, would this just mean that the posterior gap is larger and that the training time of this method would take much longer?

Limitations:
Limitations were not discussed. Example topics would include deep fakes etc.

7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

This paper proposes an approach to leverage existing pretrained unconditional diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs) as decoders within an autoencoder framework, departing from prior work (DiffAE) that employed conditional DPMs. The core contribution involves adapting classifier-guidance methodology to operate on latent semantic codes z, but rather than explicitly training classifiers to estimate p(z | x_t) and computing associated scores, the authors introduce a technique that directly regresses toward the posterior mean gap of a frozen, unsupervised pretrained DPM. Experimental results show improved reconstruction fidelity compared to DiffAE while maintaining a more streamlined training procedure.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

**Strengths:**
- The manuscript demonstrates excellent clarity in presentation, with the technical formulation presented in an accessible and logical manner
- The proposed approach elegantly sidesteps known issues in prior classifier-guidance methods regarding the scaling of guidance signals by instead fitting directly to the posterior mean gap, which represents a principled solution to this problem

**Weaknesses:**
- Section 3.4 lacks sufficient clarity regarding the identification criteria for the onset and conclusion of the critical stage. The methodology for determining these boundaries requires more explicit explanation
- Line 124 contains grammatically awkward phrasing ("Except the latent code z") that should be revised for improved readability
- The paper would benefit from ablation studies or empirical justification demonstrating whether the use of an additional DPM for modeling the latent distribution p(z) is truly indispensable. While DiffAE motivates their DPM choice by citing optimization challenges with VAE objectives, the authors do not explore whether simpler alternatives—such as a standard autoencoder paired with a fitted Gaussian mixture model over latent representations—might achieve comparable results

**Questions:**
- How sensitive is the method's performance to domain alignment between the pretrained DPM and the target task? Specifically, if the pretrained model originates from a substantially different domain, would this simply manifest as a larger posterior gap requiring extended training time, or would it introduce more fundamental limitations?

## Limitations Discussion

The paper does not address potential limitations or ethical considerations. The authors should discuss relevant concerns, such as applications in deepfake generation and other misuse scenarios.

## Recommendation

**Rating: 7 - Accept**

This is a technically sound contribution that demonstrates solid experimental validation and meaningful improvements over existing methods. The work exhibits good-to-excellent reproducibility and addresses important questions in latent variable modeling. The paper merits acceptance as a work of high impact within its sub-area with no critical unresolved concerns, though the authors should address the clarity issues and limitations discussion noted above, ideally in revision.

reviews.summary:
The authors propose an unsupervised method for data representation and reconstruction, utilizing a pre-trained unconditional diffusion probabilistic model (DMP) in order to avoid the long training time of such models.
The method incorporates a gradient estimator, that learns the posterior mean shift, which is then used as a condition in order to guide the sampling process towards the image reconstruction. The authors also propose a new weighting scheme for training the objective function. Learning the gradient estimator encourages the encoder to learn meaningful data representation, and the sampling process to produce better data reconstruction.
The authors provide quantitative and qualitative evaluations showing improvements in representation learning, image reconstruction, and image sampling.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strength:

- The problem is known to be valid and challenging, and the level of novelty is reasonable.

- The method, idea, and motivation are well explained in the paper. The authors provided a good intuition about their proposed network architecture.

- The authors well-explained their weighting method by describing their insights from an experiment that shows differences in the result with and without the classifier-guided sampling

Weaknesses:

- There is no algorithm that explains how the sampling step works in the author's method (how does the sampling-guided method is employed when using the model described in the paper).

- I suggest showing Figures 1,2 in separate locations (figure 2 should be closer to the text that describes it, along with figure 3).

- According to the quantitative results, the improvement of data representation is not significant. However, there is an improvement in the rest of the experiments, such as in unconditional sampling.

- Overall proofreading is required.
reviews.questions:
- Line 16: "and train it like a normal DMP": this causes some confusion, as the DMP part of the model is fixed. Also, the objective function aims to learn image reconstruction rather than image generation.

- It would be nice to see the results of the experiment shown in section 3.4, but when using the gradient-estimator as classifier-guided sampling, instead of a separate classifier, as done on MNIST.

- In the experiment section, the prediction of x_0 is done from x_t in only one step. What does this "one-step" mean? Isn't the whole reverse process required in order to retrieve x_0?

- It would be great to have an experiment that supports the claim that this method requires less training time than its competitors.
reviews.limitations:
The author did not address their method's limitations.
The described work has no potential negative societal impact.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

The authors also propose a new weighting scheme for training the objective function. Learning the gradient estimator encourages the encoder to learn meaningful data representation, and the sampling process to produce better data reconstruction.
The authors provide quantitative and qualitative evaluations showing improvements in representation learning, image reconstruction, and image sampling.

Strengths And Weaknesses:

Weaknesses:

Limitations:
The author did not address their method's limitations.
The described work has no potential negative societal impact.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

This paper presents an unsupervised approach to data representation learning and image reconstruction that leverages a pre-trained unconditional diffusion probabilistic model (DPM) to circumvent the computational expense associated with training such models from scratch. The core contribution involves a learnable gradient estimator that captures the posterior mean shift, which subsequently serves as a conditioning signal to direct the sampling procedure toward improved image reconstruction. Additionally, the authors introduce a novel weighting strategy for the training objective. By training this gradient estimator, the method encourages the encoder to discover salient data representations while simultaneously enabling the sampling mechanism to generate higher-quality reconstructions. The paper includes both quantitative and qualitative experimental validation demonstrating gains across representation learning, image reconstruction quality, and sampling performance.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

**Strengths:**

- The addressed problem is well-established and computationally challenging; the proposed solution demonstrates reasonable novelty for tackling it.

- The presentation of the core concepts, methodology, and underlying rationale is clear and accessible. The authors effectively communicate the intuition behind their proposed architectural design.

- The weighting scheme is well-motivated through experimental evidence, with the authors effectively illustrating the performance differences observed when the classifier-guided sampling mechanism is either included or excluded.

**Weaknesses:**

- The paper lacks a formal algorithmic description detailing the mechanics of the sampling procedure, particularly regarding how the proposed guidance mechanism is operationalized within the overall framework.

- Figures 1 and 2 should be repositioned for improved readability; specifically, Figure 2 would benefit from placement adjacent to its corresponding textual discussion, alongside Figure 3.

- The quantitative improvements in representation learning appear marginal based on the experimental results presented. While other experimental components (e.g., unconditional generation) show more substantial gains, the representation learning improvements warrant careful interpretation.

- The manuscript would benefit from comprehensive copyediting and proofreading to address various minor issues throughout.

## Questions and Clarifications

- Line 16 contains potentially ambiguous language regarding "training it like a normal DPM," which creates confusion given that the DPM component is frozen. Furthermore, the stated objective centers on image reconstruction rather than generation, which deserves clearer articulation.

- It would strengthen the work to present results from the experiment described in Section 3.4 where the learned gradient estimator functions as the guidance mechanism for classifier-guided sampling, similar to the MNIST experiments, rather than relying on a separately trained classifier.

- In the experimental section, the reconstruction of x_0 from x_t is described as occurring in "one step." This terminology requires clarification—does this not necessitate executing the complete reverse diffusion process to recover x_0?

- Empirical evidence supporting the claim that this approach demands significantly less training time relative to existing methods would substantially strengthen the contribution.

## Limitations

The authors have not adequately discussed the limitations inherent to their proposed method.

The work presents no apparent concerns regarding potential negative societal impacts.

## Overall Assessment

This submission presents technically competent work with moderate to substantial potential impact. The experimental design, resource requirements, and reproducibility considerations do not raise significant concerns. The paper merits acceptance, though addressing the raised questions and clarifications would enhance its overall quality and clarity.

**Rating: 6 – Weak Accept**: Technically sound contribution with moderate-to-high potential impact, without major reservations regarding evaluation methodology, resource utilization, reproducibility, or ethical considerations.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The authors propose an unsupervised method for data representation and reconstruction, utilizing a pre-trained unconditional diffusion probabilistic model (DMP) in order to avoid the long training time of such models.
The method incorporates a gradient estimator, that learns the posterior mean shift, which is then used as a condition in order to guide the sampling process towards the image reconstruction. The authors also propose a new weighting scheme for training the objective function. Learning the gradient estimator encourages the encoder to learn meaningful data representation, and the sampling process to produce better data reconstruction.
The authors provide quantitative and qualitative evaluations showing improvements in representation learning, image reconstruction, and image sampling.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strength:

- The problem is known to be valid and challenging, and the level of novelty is reasonable.

- The method, idea, and motivation are well explained in the paper. The authors provided a good intuition about their proposed network architecture.

- The authors well-explained their weighting method by describing their insights from an experiment that shows differences in the result with and without the classifier-guided sampling

Weaknesses:

- There is no algorithm that explains how the sampling step works in the author's method (how does the sampling-guided method is employed when using the model described in the paper).

- I suggest showing Figures 1,2 in separate locations (figure 2 should be closer to the text that describes it, along with figure 3).

- According to the quantitative results, the improvement of data representation is not significant. However, there is an improvement in the rest of the experiments, such as in unconditional sampling.

- Overall proofreading is required.

Questions:
- Line 16: "and train it like a normal DMP": this causes some confusion, as the DMP part of the model is fixed. Also, the objective function aims to learn image reconstruction rather than image generation.

- It would be nice to see the results of the experiment shown in section 3.4, but when using the gradient-estimator as classifier-guided sampling, instead of a separate classifier, as done on MNIST.

- In the experiment section, the prediction of x_0 is done from x_t in only one step. What does this "one-step" mean? Isn't the whole reverse process required in order to retrieve x_0?

- It would be great to have an experiment that supports the claim that this method requires less training time than its competitors.

Limitations:
The author did not address their method's limitations.
The described work has no potential negative societal impact.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

Summary:
The paper presents a new learning framework for diffusion probabilistic models. Unlike the most existing diffusion probabilistic models, which mainly embed the data into a predefined distribution through the Markov chain, the proposed framework aims to learn a robust representation feature through a pre-trained diffusion models. The authors also introduce a weighting scheme redesign function to further improve the representation learning in the diffusion models. Extensive experiments are conducted on multiple scenarios (e.g. FFHQ, CelebA, Bedroom).

Strengths And Weaknesses:
### Strengths
- The task of representation learning via diffusion probabilistic models is novel and interesting.
- The overall idea and the approach towards addressing it seems reasonable. 

### Weaknesses
- The paper is not well written and hard to read.
- The technical novelty is limited. Neither the designed network nor the theory are new. 
- The qualitative results are poor, and the quantitative improvement is limited.

Questions:
- What's the key difference between the proposed framework with the latest state-of-the-art diffusion autoencoders (Diff-AE) [21]. While the authors consider Diff-AE as a baseline, the two works seem to be similar. The authors should clearly explain what is the difference between them.
- Besides, The qualitative comparison to the Diff-AE baseline is missing, even after considering the results in the Appendix. What's more, the quantitative improvement in Tables 1, 2, and 3 is limited compared to the  Diff-AE baseline.
- It takes me a hard time buying the $x_0\to x_t$ in Figure 1. If I understand correctly, this should be a diffusion processing in general. Then, does this contain any pre-trained parameters?
- Could please the authors illuminate the difference between these results shown in Figure 5? While the authors claimed to "some stochastic variations", it seems to be very hard to capture such a conclusion.

Limitations are not discussed.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Summary

This work proposes a novel learning framework for diffusion probabilistic models that diverges from conventional approaches by focusing on learning robust representation features through pre-trained diffusion models, rather than simply embedding data into predefined distributions via Markov chains. The authors introduce a redesigned weighting scheme to enhance representation learning capabilities. The proposed approach is evaluated across multiple benchmark datasets including FFHQ, CelebA, and Bedroom scenes.

## Strengths

- The problem of learning meaningful representations within the diffusion probabilistic model framework is both novel and compelling.
- The fundamental approach and conceptual direction for tackling this problem appear sound and well-motivated.

## Weaknesses

- The manuscript suffers from presentation issues that impede clarity and readability throughout.
- The technical contribution is somewhat incremental, with neither the architectural components nor the theoretical foundations appearing particularly novel.
- The empirical validation is underwhelming, with both qualitative results and quantitative gains being modest in magnitude.

## Detailed Questions and Concerns

- A critical clarification is needed regarding how the proposed framework meaningfully distinguishes itself from the recent Diffusion Autoencoder (Diff-AE) work [21], which is included as a baseline. The two approaches appear quite similar in their core mechanics, and the authors must articulate precisely what differentiates their contribution.

- The experimental evaluation lacks qualitative comparisons against the Diff-AE baseline, even when considering supplementary materials. Furthermore, the quantitative improvements demonstrated in Tables 1, 2, and 3 relative to Diff-AE are marginal at best.

- The depiction of the $x_0\to x_t$ transformation in Figure 1 requires clarification. As presented, this appears to represent a standard diffusion process, but it remains unclear whether this incorporates any pre-trained model parameters and how.

- The distinctions between the results presented in Figure 5 are difficult to discern. While the authors attribute differences to "stochastic variations," the visual evidence does not convincingly support this interpretation.

## Missing Elements

- The paper does not include a discussion of its limitations, which would strengthen the overall contribution.

## Recommendation

**Rating: 5 - Borderline accept**

This paper represents technically competent work where the arguments for acceptance marginally outweigh those for rejection, though the evaluation could be more comprehensive. The core idea has merit despite limitations in novelty and empirical validation.

reviews.summary: This paper presents a method of learning representations out-of pretrained unconditional diffusion models. Different from DiffusionAE which learns the encoder and condition DPM together, this paper proposes to keep a pretrained unconditional diffusion models unchanged, and learn the gradient of classifier guidance which takes a latent variable as the additional input, and the latent variable in encoded by a learnable encoder. Empirical results show that this method leads to better reconstruction and few-shot conditional generation results compared to DiffusionAE with less training expense. Qualitatively the method learns meaningful representations, and it can also improve the sample quality of unconditional diffusion models with a learned latent DPM on the latent variables.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strength:

- This paper is well written and easy to follow. It clearly states its connection with the previous close related work, DiffusionAE, and points out the advantage over the previous work. 
- The proposed method is technically sound and naturally combines learning representation and classifier guidance. 
- Various experiments are carried out and results are compared with baseline methods, making the method more convincing. 

Weaknesses: 
- IMO the novelty of this work is kind of limited. Compared to DiffusionAE, this work includes the classifier guidance and the latent codes are embedded into the learnable guidance term instead of the diffusion model, expect for which all other components remains the same as DiffusionAE, such as reversing DDIM sampler for latent code and learning latent DPM for z. Technically speaking I don't think there's a significant novel method proposed here.
- Compared to DiffusionAE, this method includes guidance term so it is not quite surprising to me that it can outperform DiffusionAE. It's not clear to me whether the improvement is due to the guidance mechanism or due to the novel formulation $G_\psi$.
reviews.questions:
- One baseline worthwhile to compare is DiffusionAE + classifier-free guidance. If the proposed method can beat this baseline, it will be more convincing that the improvement is given by the novel formulation $G_\psi$, instead of the guidance formulation which is not the contribution of this paper. 

- For classifier(-free) guidance, it is known that results are better if the diffusion models are also conditional, i.e., the modified score is given by $\nabla_{x_t} [\log p(x_t|c) + \omega \log p(c|x_t)]$ instead of $\nabla_{x_t} [\log p(x_t) + \omega \log p(c|x_t)]$. I'm wondering if similar conclusion applies here. I.e., if you condition both the diffusion model and the classifier gradient $G_\psi$ with the latent variables $z$, whether you can get better performance than the current formulation.
reviews.limitations: No limitation and potential negative social impact are discussed .

Summary:
This paper presents a method of learning representations out-of pretrained unconditional diffusion models. Different from DiffusionAE which learns the encoder and condition DPM together, this paper proposes to keep a pretrained unconditional diffusion models unchanged, and learn the gradient of classifier guidance which takes a latent variable as the additional input, and the latent variable in encoded by a learnable encoder. Empirical results show that this method leads to better reconstruction and few-shot conditional generation results compared to DiffusionAE with less training expense. Qualitatively the method learns meaningful representations, and it can also improve the sample quality of unconditional diffusion models with a learned latent DPM on the latent variables.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strength:

- This paper is well written and easy to follow. It clearly states its connection with the previous close related work, DiffusionAE, and points out the advantage over the previous work. 
- The proposed method is technically sound and naturally combines learning representation and classifier guidance. 
- Various experiments are carried out and results are compared with baseline methods, making the method more convincing. 

Weaknesses: 
- IMO the novelty of this work is kind of limited. Compared to DiffusionAE, this work includes the classifier guidance and the latent codes are embedded into the learnable guidance term instead of the diffusion model, expect for which all other components remains the same as DiffusionAE, such as reversing DDIM sampler for latent code and learning latent DPM for z. Technically speaking I don't think there's a significant novel method proposed here.
- Compared to DiffusionAE, this method includes guidance term so it is not quite surprising to me that it can outperform DiffusionAE. It's not clear to me whether the improvement is due to the guidance mechanism or due to the novel formulation $G_\psi$.

Questions:
- One baseline worthwhile to compare is DiffusionAE + classifier-free guidance. If the proposed method can beat this baseline, it will be more convincing that the improvement is given by the novel formulation $G_\psi$, instead of the guidance formulation which is not the contribution of this paper. 

- For classifier(-free) guidance, it is known that results are better if the diffusion models are also conditional, i.e., the modified score is given by $\nabla_{x_t} [\log p(x_t|c) + \omega \log p(c|x_t)]$ instead of $\nabla_{x_t} [\log p(x_t) + \omega \log p(c|x_t)]$. I'm wondering if similar conclusion applies here. I.e., if you condition both the diffusion model and the classifier gradient $G_\psi$ with the latent variables $z$, whether you can get better performance than the current formulation.

Limitations:
No limitation and potential negative social impact are discussed .

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Summary

The paper introduces a novel approach for extracting representations from pretrained unconditional diffusion models by learning classifier guidance gradients conditioned on learnable latent codes. Unlike DiffusionAE, which jointly trains the encoder alongside a conditional diffusion model, this work maintains a frozen pretrained unconditional diffusion model and instead learns a guidance function parameterized by an encoder-generated latent variable. The experimental evaluation demonstrates improvements in reconstruction fidelity and few-shot conditional generation performance relative to DiffusionAE, while requiring substantially less computational overhead. The learned representations appear semantically meaningful, and the method can additionally enhance sample quality in the original unconditional diffusion model through a learned latent diffusion process.

## Strengths

- The paper is clearly articulated with good exposition of the problem space. The authors effectively contextualize their contribution relative to DiffusionAE and transparently discuss the comparative advantages of their approach.

- The technical approach is sound and represents a natural integration of representation learning with the classifier guidance framework.

- A comprehensive experimental evaluation is provided with comparisons against relevant baselines, which strengthens the empirical validation of the proposed method.

## Weaknesses

- The scope of novelty appears somewhat constrained. The core distinction from DiffusionAE centers on incorporating classifier guidance and placing latent codes within the guidance function rather than the diffusion model itself. Beyond this modification, the methodology largely mirrors DiffusionAE's architecture, including the use of reversed DDIM sampling for latent code inference and a learned latent diffusion model. From a technical perspective, the incremental contribution feels limited.

- The performance gains over DiffusionAE are partially attributable to the inclusion of guidance, which is not a novel contribution in this work. It remains unclear whether observed improvements stem from the guidance mechanism per se or from the specific formulation of $G_\psi$. This ambiguity weakens the claim regarding the method's distinctive contribution.

## Questions and Suggestions

- A valuable ablation would involve comparing against DiffusionAE augmented with classifier-free guidance. If the proposed method substantially outperforms this variant, it would provide stronger evidence that improvements derive from the novel $G_\psi$ formulation rather than simply from the addition of guidance, which is a known technique.

- In the classifier guidance literature, conditioning the base diffusion model (i.e., using $\nabla_{x_t} [\log p(x_t|c) + \omega \log p(c|x_t)]$ rather than $\nabla_{x_t} [\log p(x_t) + \omega \log p(c|x_t)]$) typically yields superior results. The authors should investigate whether a similar principle applies to their framework—specifically, whether jointly conditioning both the diffusion model and the guidance gradient $G_\psi$ on the latent variables $z$ would yield improvements over the current formulation.

## Limitations

The paper does not discuss its limitations or address potential negative societal implications of the work.

## Recommendation

**5: Borderline accept** - The paper presents a technically competent contribution with solid experimental results, where the strengths sufficiently outweigh the limitations to merit acceptance. However, the constrained novelty and questions regarding the source of empirical improvements prevent a stronger recommendation.

title: S$^3$-NeRF: Neural Reflectance Field from Shading and Shadow under a Single Viewpoint

reviews.summary:
This pape proposed a method that aims at solving the near-field photometric stereo via neural reflectance field representation. The core insight of this paper is to use both the shading and cast-shadows of the view as the cues to recover both the visible and invisible parts of a scene. 
The paper adopts the NeRF-like neural field representation to represent the occupancy field, reflectance field of a scene. They then use lighting, BRDF modeling and shadow rendering to reconstruct the observed images. The networks are jointly optimized via volume rendering loss, smoothness loss and color loss.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths
- The proposed method can estimate the geometry of the invisible region of the scene from a single-view point. The paper is interesting in estimating invisible regions from the shadows. And unlike previous shadow-based methods, this paper doesn't require explicitly shadow detection as input.
- The method presents state-of-the-art geometry reconstruction results compare to prior near-field photometric stereo methods. 
Each component of the method, such as shadow and shading, is validated in the ablation studies.
- The paper is well writen and easy to follow. 

Weaknesses
- Each part of the proposed method is not novel. The method consists of three major parts: neural reflectance field representation follows [38] in occupancy field estimation and normal computation; the color and BRDF modeling follow [15,24] to use the sphere gaussian bases, SG weights and diffuse albedo; the shadow computation is slightly different to [48], but it is similar to [24] where they both check the visibility of the surface point. Hence, in the proposed method and rendering procedure of this paper, the novelty is very limited.

- It is suggested to evaluate this method in more challenging real and synthetic datasets. The method was only tested on two datasets: a simple synthetic dataset and a real dataset. For the synthetic dataset: the background is clean and uniform; how will this paper perform in backgrounds with more textures?

- I am curious to see how the number of lights affects the accuracy of the geometry in the invisible part of the scene. The only cues for invisible regions are the casted shadows on the ground. If lights fail to illuminate the invisible regions from different angles, how will the accuracy be affected?
reviews.questions: Please see the quesitons in the Weaknesses part.
reviews.limitations:
- Limitations are well discussed in the paper.
- Suggestions: It will be great to isolate the invisible region of the scene and show quantitatively how the proposed shadow rendering improve the accuracy in these regions.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.

reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

This work presents an approach for addressing near-field photometric stereo through neural reflectance field modeling. The central contribution involves leveraging both shading information and shadow artifacts as complementary signals to reconstruct both visible and occluded regions of a scene.

The authors employ a NeRF-inspired neural field framework to model occupancy and reflectance properties. These representations are then combined with explicit lighting and BRDF formulations to render observed images. The overall system is trained through a combination of volume rendering, smoothness, and color consistency objectives.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

### Strengths

- A notable contribution is the ability to infer geometry in occluded regions from a single viewpoint by exploiting shadow information. The use of cast shadows to recover hidden geometry is conceptually interesting, and the approach improves upon prior shadow-based techniques by avoiding the need for explicit shadow segmentation as a preprocessing step.

- The experimental results demonstrate competitive performance relative to existing near-field photometric stereo baselines. The ablation studies effectively validate the contribution of individual components, including both shadow and shading terms.

- The paper is clearly written and straightforward to follow, making the methodology accessible to readers.

### Weaknesses

- The individual technical components lack significant novelty. The approach combines three established elements: the occupancy field estimation and normal derivation draw from [38]; the BRDF and color modeling utilize established techniques from [15,24] involving spherical Gaussian bases, SG weighting schemes, and diffuse albedo parameters; the shadow computation, while slightly modified from [48], remains conceptually similar to the visibility checking procedure in [24]. Consequently, the overall novelty of both the methodology and the rendering pipeline is limited.

- The experimental validation would benefit from evaluation on more diverse and challenging datasets. The current evaluation is restricted to two datasets—a relatively simple synthetic dataset with uniform, uncluttered backgrounds, and one real dataset. It remains unclear how the method would perform on synthetic datasets featuring complex background textures or more challenging visual conditions.

- A significant open question concerns the sensitivity of the approach to the number and angular distribution of light sources. Since the primary signal for reconstructing occluded regions comes from shadows cast onto visible surfaces, the method's performance may degrade if the lighting configuration fails to illuminate the hidden regions from sufficiently varied angles. The impact of this limitation on reconstruction accuracy deserves investigation.

## Questions

The questions raised are addressed in the weaknesses section above.

## Limitations

- The paper provides adequate discussion of its limitations.
- Suggestion: It would strengthen the work to isolate the occluded regions and provide quantitative metrics demonstrating the specific improvements that shadow rendering contributes to accuracy in these regions.

## Recommendation

**6: Weak Accept** – The paper presents technically sound work with moderate-to-high potential impact. There are no significant concerns regarding evaluation methodology, computational requirements, reproducibility, or ethical considerations.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This pape proposed a method that aims at solving the near-field photometric stereo via neural reflectance field representation. The core insight of this paper is to use both the shading and cast-shadows of the view as the cues to recover both the visible and invisible parts of a scene. 
The paper adopts the NeRF-like neural field representation to represent the occupancy field, reflectance field of a scene. They then use lighting, BRDF modeling and shadow rendering to reconstruct the observed images. The networks are jointly optimized via volume rendering loss, smoothness loss and color loss.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths
- The proposed method can estimate the geometry of the invisible region of the scene from a single-view point. The paper is interesting in estimating invisible regions from the shadows. And unlike previous shadow-based methods, this paper doesn't require explicitly shadow detection as input.
- The method presents state-of-the-art geometry reconstruction results compare to prior near-field photometric stereo methods. 
Each component of the method, such as shadow and shading, is validated in the ablation studies.
- The paper is well writen and easy to follow. 

Weaknesses
- Each part of the proposed method is not novel. The method consists of three major parts: neural reflectance field representation follows [38] in occupancy field estimation and normal computation; the color and BRDF modeling follow [15,24] to use the sphere gaussian bases, SG weights and diffuse albedo; the shadow computation is slightly different to [48], but it is similar to [24] where they both check the visibility of the surface point. Hence, in the proposed method and rendering procedure of this paper, the novelty is very limited.

- It is suggested to evaluate this method in more challenging real and synthetic datasets. The method was only tested on two datasets: a simple synthetic dataset and a real dataset. For the synthetic dataset: the background is clean and uniform; how will this paper perform in backgrounds with more textures?

- I am curious to see how the number of lights affects the accuracy of the geometry in the invisible part of the scene. The only cues for invisible regions are the casted shadows on the ground. If lights fail to illuminate the invisible regions from different angles, how will the accuracy be affected?

Questions:
Please see the quesitons in the Weaknesses part.

Limitations:
- Limitations are well discussed in the paper.
- Suggestions: It will be great to isolate the invisible region of the scene and show quantitatively how the proposed shadow rendering improve the accuracy in these regions.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.original_rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

This paper proposed a single-view scene reconstruction method under different point light conditions based on the neural reflectance field. It uses the inverse-square law and BRDF to model the shading and root finding to model the shadow. By explicitly utilizing both shading and shadow cues, the model can reconstruct the scene from single view, even for some invisible regions.

reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
### Strengths

A novel NeRF-based method that faithfully reconstructs a scene from a fixed viewpoint with multiple single point light conditions by explicitly modeling the shading and shadow. By carefully modeling the single point light shading by inverse-square law and BRDF, and shadow by root-finding. This method gives an excellent result.
The paper clearly illustrated the method in detail. The authors compared their method with other methods quantitatively and qualitatively. They also evaluated their model with multiple occluding objects, showing it can reconstruct the unseen area with shading and shadow cues.
 
### Weakness

Summary:
This paper proposed a single-view scene reconstruction method under different point light conditions based on the neural reflectance field. It uses the inverse-square law and BRDF to model the shading and root finding to model the shadow. By explicitly utilizing both shading and shadow cues, the model can reconstruct the scene from single view, even for some invisible regions.
The authors evaluated the model with different objects and showed that the method can well reconstruct the geometry with images from single view but different point lights.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
### Strengths

A novel NeRF-based method that faithfully reconstructs a scene from a fixed viewpoint with multiple single point light conditions by explicitly modeling the shading and shadow. By carefully modeling the single point light shading by inverse-square law and BRDF, and shadow by root-finding. This method gives an excellent result.
The paper clearly illustrated the method in detail. The authors compared their method with other methods quantitatively and qualitatively. They also evaluated their model with multiple occluding objects, showing it can reconstruct the unseen area with shading and shadow cues.
 
### Weakness

The authors studied the contribution of different components (shading and shadow) of their method. But the impact of different loss terms was not included. Specially, at L171, how the value of $\alpha$ for normal smoothness loss is found is not illustrated.

Questions:
How’s the normal smoothness loss affect your method? Is that similar to UNISURF? From your result, especially on LEGO, the loss of detail of studs and track seems very serious. Is that caused by normal smoothness loss or by more complicated light reflection at those areas?

Limitations:
Yes, the authors addressed different aspect of limitations of the work in the paper and supplementary materials. They also pointed out this method can recover some non-directly seen area, that may address some privacy issues.

Rating:
8: Strong Accept: Technically strong paper, with novel ideas, excellent impact on at least one area, or high-to-excellent impact on multiple areas, with excellent evaluation, resources, and reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

This work presents a novel approach for reconstructing 3D scenes from a single viewpoint across multiple point light illumination conditions using neural reflectance fields. The method explicitly incorporates physical light transport principles, employing the inverse-square law and BRDF models to capture shading effects, while utilizing root-finding algorithms to handle shadow computation. Through the joint exploitation of shading and shadow information, the approach enables scene reconstruction including regions not directly visible from the camera viewpoint. Experimental validation demonstrates that the technique achieves strong geometric reconstruction quality across diverse objects using only single-view images captured under varying point light sources.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

### Strengths

The paper introduces an innovative NeRF-based framework that successfully reconstructs scenes from a fixed camera position using multiple single-point illumination conditions. The explicit modeling of light transport phenomena—particularly the careful parameterization of point light shading through inverse-square law and BRDF principles, combined with shadow computation via root-finding—yields compelling reconstruction results. This physically-motivated design is a significant contribution to the field.

The technical exposition is thorough and well-articulated, with the methodology presented in considerable detail. The authors provide both quantitative and qualitative comparisons against baseline approaches, strengthening the empirical validation. Notably, experiments involving multiple occluding objects convincingly demonstrate the method's capacity to infer geometry in occluded regions by leveraging shading and shadow cues as geometric priors.

### Weaknesses

While the authors conduct ablation studies examining the individual contributions of shading and shadow components, the analysis does not extend to investigating the impact of individual loss function terms. Specifically, the paper lacks clarity regarding how the hyperparameter α controlling the normal smoothness loss (referenced at L171) was determined or selected.

## Questions and Concerns

What is the quantitative influence of the normal smoothness regularization on reconstruction quality? Does this regularization strategy parallel the approach used in UNISURF? Examining the results, particularly the LEGO dataset, reveals noticeable loss of fine details in structures such as studs and tracks. Can the authors clarify whether this degradation stems from the normal smoothness loss or rather from the inherent complexity of capturing high-frequency reflectance properties under the employed lighting model?

## Limitations

The authors adequately address various limitations throughout the main manuscript and supplementary materials. They also thoughtfully discuss how the method's ability to reconstruct occluded regions could have positive implications for privacy-preserving applications, which represents an interesting secondary consideration.

## Overall Assessment and Recommendation

**Rating: 8 - Strong Accept**

This is a technically sound paper that introduces novel ideas with strong impact across multiple dimensions. The work demonstrates excellent experimental rigor with comprehensive evaluation and reproducibility considerations. No significant ethical concerns remain unaddressed. The physically-grounded approach to single-view reconstruction under varying illumination represents a meaningful advance in the field, and the results validate the effectiveness of the proposed methodology.

reviews.summary: This paper is about using neural radiance field to reconstruction the geometry of an object lit by varying directional light sources, leveraging cues of cast shadow. The optimization uses an efficient way to trace rays for shadow computation.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses: The setup is interesting as in using shadow cues to reconstruct 3D geometry when multi-view captures are not available. The scope of the paper is quite limited, in terms of the problem setup and the practicality. The setup is demanding -- real directional light plus known lighting direction. This highly controlled lighting setup seems to only be feasible in a laboratory or a light stage setting. This may be the reason the paper only shows toy synthetic examples. Another 'contrived' setup is in the simple scene / background. Cast shadow depends on the object geometry, the lighting, and also the surface the shadow is cast on. The geometry of the surface and the geometry of the object can be entangled to cause ambiguity. This is more severe in real-world examples. The task can be unnecessarily (due to such setup being unlikely in real-world scenarios) challenging and ambiguous without any priors on the object geometry. The reconstructed geometry is not on par with multi-view settings, and degrades drastically as the number of lights decreases. My biggest concern about this paper is it makes the reconstruction task unnecessarily challenging and impractical.
reviews.questions:
- to make the setup slightly more practical, how well does the method perform when shadow is softer, i.e., slightly larger light source, or small area lights?

- line 149: has the proposed shadow generation method been validated over direct regression method using an MLP?

- fig 4: should also show the nearest neighbor training samples
reviews.limitations:
The calibrated lighting is more demanding than calibrated camera pose, which can be easily acquired, even though not entirely accurate, on most modern smartphones. The lighting needs to be real directional light source, which is difficult to acquire in real-world scenarios.

It would be also valuable to discuss potential applications for the proposed setup and algorithm.

Strengths And Weaknesses:

The scope of the paper is quite limited, in terms of the problem setup and the practicality. The setup is demanding -- real directional light plus known lighting direction. This highly controlled lighting setup seems to only be feasible in a laboratory or a light stage setting. This may be the reason the paper only shows toy synthetic examples. Another 'contrived' setup is in the simple scene / background. Cast shadow depends on the object geometry, the lighting, and also the surface the shadow is cast on. The task can be unnecessarily (due to such setup being unlikely in real-world scenarios) challenging and ambiguous without any priors on the object geometry. reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

This work proposes a neural radiance field approach for recovering 3D object geometry from images captured under varying directional illumination, with a particular emphasis on leveraging cast shadow information as a reconstruction signal. The method incorporates an optimized ray-tracing strategy to efficiently compute shadows during the optimization process.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

The core concept of exploiting shadow cues for geometric reconstruction in the absence of multi-view data presents an interesting research direction. However, the practical applicability and scope of this work are significantly constrained. The experimental setup imposes stringent requirements—specifically, the need for true directional light sources with precisely known directions. Such controlled conditions are typically only achievable in specialized environments such as laboratory facilities or professional light stages, which explains the paper's reliance on synthetic datasets rather than real-world demonstrations. Additionally, the simplified scene configuration—with minimal background complexity—represents another departure from realistic scenarios. Since cast shadows are jointly determined by object geometry, lighting parameters, and the receiving surface geometry, these factors can become conflated in ways that introduce substantial geometric ambiguity. This ambiguity is particularly problematic in practical settings and becomes even more acute without incorporating prior knowledge about object structure. The resulting reconstructions fall short of what multi-view approaches can achieve and show marked performance degradation as the number of light sources is reduced. The fundamental concern is that the proposed setup introduces unnecessary complexity and impracticality into the reconstruction problem.

## Questions and Suggestions

- To move toward greater practical viability, how does the method perform under softer shadow conditions—for instance, when using slightly larger light sources or area light approximations?

- Regarding line 149: Has the proposed shadow computation strategy been empirically compared against a baseline approach using direct MLP-based regression?

- In Figure 4: It would be informative to also include visualizations of the nearest neighbor samples from the training set for reference.

## Limitations Discussion

The requirement for calibrated lighting parameters represents a more demanding constraint than camera pose calibration, which can be obtained with reasonable accuracy using contemporary smartphone technology. The necessity for true directional light sources constitutes a significant practical limitation in real-world deployment scenarios.

Furthermore, a more thorough discussion of potential real-world applications suited to this particular setup and algorithmic approach would strengthen the work's contribution.

## Recommendation

**4: Borderline reject**: This paper demonstrates technical competence and methodological soundness; however, the limitations in experimental scope and evaluation breadth present stronger arguments against acceptance than the merits of the approach present in its favor. The restricted practical applicability and reliance on synthetic data are primary concerns.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper is about using neural radiance field to reconstruction the geometry of an object lit by varying directional light sources, leveraging cues of cast shadow. The optimization uses an efficient way to trace rays for shadow computation.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
The setup is interesting as in using shadow cues to reconstruct 3D geometry when multi-view captures are not available. The scope of the paper is quite limited, in terms of the problem setup and the practicality. The setup is demanding -- real directional light plus known lighting direction. This highly controlled lighting setup seems to only be feasible in a laboratory or a light stage setting. This may be the reason the paper only shows toy synthetic examples. Another 'contrived' setup is in the simple scene / background. Cast shadow depends on the object geometry, the lighting, and also the surface the shadow is cast on. The geometry of the surface and the geometry of the object can be entangled to cause ambiguity. This is more severe in real-world examples. The task can be unnecessarily (due to such setup being unlikely in real-world scenarios) challenging and ambiguous without any priors on the object geometry. The reconstructed geometry is not on par with multi-view settings, and degrades drastically as the number of lights decreases. My biggest concern about this paper is it makes the reconstruction task unnecessarily challenging and impractical.

Questions:
- to make the setup slightly more practical, how well does the method perform when shadow is softer, i.e., slightly larger light source, or small area lights?

- line 149: has the proposed shadow generation method been validated over direct regression method using an MLP?

- fig 4: should also show the nearest neighbor training samples

Limitations:
The calibrated lighting is more demanding than calibrated camera pose, which can be easily acquired, even though not entirely accurate, on most modern smartphones. The lighting needs to be real directional light source, which is difficult to acquire in real-world scenarios.

It would be also valuable to discuss potential applications for the proposed setup and algorithm.

Rating:
4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.
