Following in the tradition of physics-informed neural networks (PINNs), the flow equation is parametrized by a neural network. The parameters of the neural network are optimised by replacing a hand-tuned loss function such as the mean-squared error, with a discriminator and an adversarial loss. This method appears to provide improved performance over PINNs trained with hand-tuned loss functions and numerical solvers, on several well-known differential equations.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
1) The paper is very well-written, well-structured and easy to follow. The method and results, for the most part, are described in clear detail.
2) The ablation study, and discussion of methods to ensure stable training were extremely interesting and relevant, given that GANs are known to be difficult to train.
2) The extensive results in the paper make a convincing case for using GANs over other methods for solving differential equations -- in particular, with respect to the variability of the solutions from other methods across different problems.

Weaknesses:
1) Many of the details of training, hyperparameters etc., included in the methods section (sections 3.2.1, 3.2.2, 3.4), while important for reproducibility, distract from the main message of the paper, and hinder readability.
2) Some of the results require a lot more elucidation: e.g. the details of the ablation study in section 5.2 are mostly relegated to the appendix, and thus make the import of the results hard to parse; the plots in Figure 3 are barely mentioned or described in the text. 
3) The paper could improve with a more extensive discussion of the limitations of the method (the conclusion mentions non-interpretability of the discriminator, but not much more).
4) The paper claims to present two "methods" to improve robustness in training: residual monitoring and addition of instance noise. While it is important to document the techniques used to stabilised training, neither of these are nescessarily novel contributions in the context of GANs. Furthermore, it is not clear whether these measures would be equally effective without spectral normalisation, skip connections in the generator, or with different choice of variance for the instance noise, or a change in the 25% training iteration threshold for residual monitoring.

Questions:
As outlined in the previous section, I believe the paper makes a strong case for using GANs to solve differential equations. Some concrete suggestions to address the weaknesses:
1) It would be good to move the details of sections 3.2.1, 3.2.2 and 3.4 to the appendix, and move details of the ablation study to the main paper. Furthermore, sections 3.3 and 4.1 describe training / engineering choices to a level of detail that hinders readability, and can certainly be condensed.
2) It would be good to have a more extensive discussion of Figure 3. Moreover, Figure 3 appears to show only simulations from the GAN -- it would be good to have some examples in of the simulations from other methods as well, in order to contextualize the GAN performance beyond the reported MSE numbers.
3) While the methods residual monitoring and instance noise are certainly important details for reproducibility, it is not convincing that they would work without the other hyperparameter or architectural choices. It would therefore be good to modify the discussion of these two "methods", and to present them as potential avenues for stable training rather than a contribution of the paper.

Questions:
1) The performance of the different methods for solving differential equations are compared solely on the basis of MSE. Is there a particular reason for choosing this metric? -- it is particularly surprising that there is no justification provided for this in the paper, since the introduction makes a strong point that the choice of similarity metric typically used to optimize parameters for the PINNs are arbitrary. It would also be good to see whether the GAN performance is equally good under other similarity metrics e.g. L1, or MMD 
2) In line 181-182, "We use the ReLU function...noise should not be used." -- this logic is not clear. Why should $L_g < L_d$ on any particular iteration indicate a better generator? -- this could also be due to initialisation, especially at the beginning of training.
3) How does the coarseness of the grid from which $t, x$ are sampled affect the performance of the GANs over other methods?

Minor comments:
1) The notation in section 4 is confusing in places: $G(x)$ and $\Psi_{\theta}(x)$ are used somewhat interchangeably; in line 179-181 $L_g$ and $L_d$ are not defined.
2) In Figure 2, a legend is not required in every panel
3) In Figure 3, the dotted lines in (a) and (b) are note identified in the legend; the colourbars have no label in (c) and (d)

Limitations:
It would be good if the discussion could address the following issues:
1) While the discriminator is not interpretable, the generator has potentially the same issues. What scientific insight is gained from an (effectively) black box generator modeling a flow equation?
2) While the GANs do provide an improvement over existing methods in terms of performance, it would be good to have an idea of the associated computational costs, sample efficiency and training times compared to the other methods.
3) How does this method scale with the number of dynamical variables, the size of the coarseness of the $t, x$ grid?

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Summary

This paper introduces a GAN-based approach for solving ordinary and partial differential equations, building upon the physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) framework. Rather than relying on manually-tuned loss functions like mean-squared error, the method employs a discriminator network and adversarial training to optimize the neural network parameters that represent the solution. The empirical evaluation demonstrates superior performance relative to both conventional PINNs and traditional numerical solvers across multiple benchmark differential equation problems.

## Strengths

1) The manuscript demonstrates excellent presentation quality with clear organization and logical flow. The methodology and findings are communicated with appropriate clarity and sufficient technical detail throughout most of the paper.

2) The inclusion of an ablation study alongside substantive discussion of training stability mechanisms is particularly valuable and timely, given the well-documented challenges associated with GAN training procedures.

3) The comprehensive experimental evaluation provides compelling evidence for the advantages of the GAN-based approach, particularly in demonstrating more consistent performance across diverse problem instances compared to alternative solution methods.

## Weaknesses

1) Sections 3.2.1, 3.2.2, and 3.4 contain extensive implementation details and hyperparameter specifications that, while essential for reproducibility, interrupt the narrative flow and detract from the core contributions. The level of technical minutiae in these sections compromises overall readability.

2) Several key results lack adequate exposition: the ablation study findings in Section 5.2 are predominantly confined to supplementary materials, making their significance difficult to assess; similarly, Figure 3's visualizations receive minimal textual explanation and contextual discussion.

3) The treatment of methodological limitations is insufficient. While the conclusion briefly mentions discriminator interpretability, a more thorough examination of the method's constraints and failure modes would strengthen the work.

4) The paper identifies residual monitoring and instance noise addition as two "methods" for enhancing training robustness. However, these techniques represent established practices in the GAN literature rather than novel contributions. Furthermore, the analysis does not adequately investigate whether these stabilization approaches would remain effective if decoupled from spectral normalization, generator skip connections, or alternative choices for instance noise variance and the 25% iteration threshold for residual monitoring.

## Specific Recommendations

1) Transfer the granular implementation details from Sections 3.2.1, 3.2.2, and 3.4 to the appendix, while promoting the ablation study results to the main manuscript. Additionally, condense the training and design discussions in Sections 3.3 and 4.1 to improve readability without sacrificing necessary information.

2) Expand the discussion surrounding Figure 3 substantially. The figure should include comparative visualizations from competing methods alongside the GAN results to provide proper context for the performance advantages beyond the reported MSE values.

3) Reframe the discussion of residual monitoring and instance noise as potentially useful strategies for achieving stable training rather than as novel methodological contributions. Acknowledge that their effectiveness is contingent upon the specific architectural and hyperparameter choices employed.

## Questions for Authors

1) The comparative evaluation relies exclusively on MSE as the performance metric. Given the paper's introduction emphasizing the arbitrariness of similarity metrics in PINN optimization, what justifies this choice? It would be instructive to evaluate GAN performance under alternative metrics such as L1 distance or maximum mean discrepancy to demonstrate robustness across different similarity measures.

2) The reasoning in lines 181-182 regarding ReLU activation and noise application requires clarification. Why should the condition $L_g < L_d$ on any given training iteration serve as a reliable indicator of generator quality, particularly given potential confounding effects from initialization and early-stage training dynamics?

3) How does the sampling grid resolution (both temporal and spatial) influence the relative performance gap between GANs and competing approaches? This parameter's sensitivity warrants investigation.

## Minor Issues

1) Notation in Section 4 contains ambiguities: $G(x)$ and $\Psi_{\theta}(x)$ are used interchangeably without clear distinction; $L_g$ and $L_d$ are referenced in lines 179-181 without explicit definition.

2) Figure 2 includes redundant legends across multiple panels that could be consolidated.

3) Figure 3 has incomplete labeling: dotted lines in panels (a) and (b) lack legend entries; colorbars in panels (c) and (d) are unlabeled.

## Discussion of Limitations

The paper would benefit from addressing the following concerns:

1) Beyond the acknowledged non-interpretability of the discriminator, the generator itself presents similar opacity concerns. What interpretable scientific understanding emerges from employing a largely black-box generator to model solution dynamics?

2) While performance improvements over existing methods are demonstrated, the paper lacks quantitative comparison of computational requirements, sample efficiency, and wall-clock training times relative to alternative approaches.

3) The scalability characteristics remain unclear: how does the method perform as the number of dynamical variables increases, or as the spatiotemporal grid becomes finer?

## Overall Assessment

This is a technically sound contribution that makes a meaningful case for applying adversarial training to differential equation solving. The experimental work is thorough and the presentation is generally strong. While the work would benefit from the organizational and clarification improvements outlined above, these represent refinements rather than fundamental deficiencies. The paper demonstrates solid technical quality with significant impact potential within its domain and merits publication.

**Rating: 7 - Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.**

It would also be good to see whether the GAN performance is equally good under other similarity metrics e.g. L1, or MMD

3) How does the coarseness of the grid from which $t, x$ are sampled affect the performance of the GANs over other methods?

Minor comments:
1) The notation in section 4 is confusing in places: $G(x)$ and $\Psi_{\theta}(x)$ are used somewhat interchangeably; in line 179-181 $L_g$ and $L_d$ are not defined.
2) In Figure 2, a legend is not required in every panel
3) In Figure 3, the dotted lines in (a) and (b) are note identified in the legend; the colourbars have no label in (c) and (d)

Limitations:
It would be good if the discussion could address the following issues:
1) While the discriminator is not interpretable, the generator has potentially the same issues. What scientific insight is gained from an (effectively) black box generator modeling a flow equation?
2) While the GANs do provide an improvement over existing methods in terms of performance, it would be good to have an idea of the associated computational costs, sample efficiency and training times compared to the other methods.
3) How does this method scale with the number of dynamical variables, the size of the coarseness of the $t, x$ grid?

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary
This work introduces a novel approach to solving differential equations by employing GAN-based adversarial training to automatically learn appropriate loss functions for Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs).

## Strengths and Weaknesses

### Strengths
- Introduces an innovative framework that combines GANs with differential equation solving in a fully unsupervised manner, eliminating the need for ground truth solutions during training.
- Demonstrates substantial empirical improvements on differential equation benchmarks, with performance gains spanning multiple orders of magnitude.
- Incorporates several well-established stabilization techniques in Section 3 to ensure robust GAN training convergence.
- The manuscript is clearly written with comprehensive experimental details provided in the supplementary materials.

### Weaknesses
- The paper lacks any treatment of mode collapse, a well-known challenge in GAN training. While the authors apply GANs in a novel context, discussing whether mode collapse could lead to multiple distinct solutions for certain equations would strengthen the work.
- The work provides neither theoretical analysis nor formal guarantees regarding convergence or solution quality. Although GANs inherently lack strong theoretical foundations, even preliminary theoretical insights would significantly enhance the contribution.

## Questions and Suggestions
- An ablation study would be valuable to assess the individual contributions of the various training stabilization techniques employed. Some of these methods may prove unnecessary, and their relative importance should be clarified.
- The authors should provide explicit commentary on the potential for mode collapse in their framework, addressing whether the method could discover multiple valid solutions and how this relates to the underlying differential equations.

## Limitations
The authors adequately discuss relevant limitations in their conclusion section. The work raises no significant ethical or societal concerns in its current form.

## Decision

**Accept (Rating: 7)**: This is a technically sound paper that makes a high-impact contribution to at least one specialized area, with strong evaluation, good reproducibility, and appropriate treatment of ethical considerations. The empirical results are compelling and the methodology is novel, though the work would benefit from theoretical grounding and more thorough ablation analysis.

reviews.summary: This work proposes Differential Equation GAN (DEQGAN) for solving differential equations using generative adversarial networks (GAN) where the generator learns to output the solution and the discriminator tries to learn a good loss function. The authors compare DEQGAN against classical PINNs over twelve ordinary differential equations (ODEs) and partial differential equations (PDEs). The experimental results show that DEQGAN can achieve multiple orders of magnitude lower L2 error than PINNs.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths: the paper is well-written and easy to follow. DEQGAN demonstrates superior empirical results compared to classical PINNs. The fact that DEQGAN doesn't depend on predefined distance such as L1, L2 can be useful in some problems.

Weakness:
1. The Related Work section misses some important discussion [1, 2]. The authors should discuss the relationship to works and place the contribution of DEQGAN in the context of prior works. For example, SA-PINNs [1] can also be viewed as learning a loss function.
2. The authors state that the L2 loss lacks theoretical justification but miss the important explanation on why and when L2 loss can be problematic. Furthermore, DEQGAN do not have a theoretical justification of the learned loss function either.
3. The loss function in DEQGAN is borrowed from generative adversarial network (GAN). The original GAN work designs its loss function such that generator is minimizing Jensen-Shannon divergence between the generated distribution and target distribution given optimal discriminator dynamics. However, the Jensen-Shannon divergence doesn't make sense in DEQGAN because the problem is to solve a differential equation with unique solution. Even with Gaussian instance noise, minimizing the divergence between two Gaussians will just be minimizing the L2 loss. I do not see why a generative model is needed here.
4. The equations considered in the experiments are artificial. Only one specific initial condition is used for each equation, which raise the concern that the authors may pick the initial conditions to put DEQGAN in advantage in the experimental results.
5. DEQGAN has training instability issue. To solve one equation, it needs to repeat runs multiple times and filters out the ones with poor performances.

[1] McClenny, Levi, and Ulisses Braga-Neto. "Self-adaptive physics-informed neural networks using a soft attention mechanism." arXiv preprint arXiv:2009.04544 (2020).
[2] Daw, Arka, M. Maruf, and Anuj Karpatne. "PID-GAN: A GAN Framework based on a Physics-informed Discriminator for Uncertainty Quantification with Physics." Proceedings of the 27th ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery & Data Mining. 2021.
reviews.questions:
1. The authors motivate the need of learning loss function by saying that L2,L1 loss functions lack theoretical justification. However, the author challenges the standard practice in physics-informed learning without explaining why and when L1, L2 loss functions can be bad choices. If L2 loss is problematic, how is the learned loss function better than L2 loss? Can the authors provide theoretical justification for it?
2. How fast is DEQGAN compared to PINNs and numerical solvers? Can authors provide time complexity comparison against PINNs and its improved variants such as SA-PINNs?
3. Only one specific artificial initial condition is used for each equation. How will DEQGAN perform if the initial condition is a random function sampled from a random field?
reviews.limitations:
1. Lack of theoretical justification of the loss function and how the disciminator can learn a better objective function.
2. DEQGAN has training instability issue. To solve one equation, it needs to repeat runs multiple times and filters out the ones with poor performances.
3. The equations considered in the experiments are artificial. Only one specific initial condition is used for each equation, which raise the concern that the authors may pick the initial conditions to put DEQGAN in advantage in the experimental results.
reviews.rating: 3: Reject: For instance, a paper with technical flaws, weak evaluation, inadequate reproducibility and incompletely addressed ethical considerations.

However, the Jensen-Shannon divergence doesn't make sense in DEQGAN because the problem is to solve a differential equation with unique solution. Even with Gaussian instance noise, minimizing the divergence between two Gaussians will just be minimizing the L2 loss. I do not see why a generative model is needed here.

3: Reject: For instance, a paper with technical flaws, weak evaluation, inadequate reproducibility and incompletely addressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Differential Equation GAN (DEQGAN)

## Summary

The paper introduces DEQGAN, a novel approach that leverages generative adversarial networks to solve differential equations. In this framework, the generator produces solutions to differential equations while the discriminator simultaneously learns an appropriate loss function. The authors benchmark their method against classical Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) using a test suite of twelve ODEs and PDEs, reporting substantial improvements with error reductions spanning multiple orders of magnitude.

## Strengths

The manuscript is clearly written and readily comprehensible. The empirical performance of DEQGAN demonstrates marked improvements over baseline PINN approaches. Additionally, the method's ability to circumvent reliance on predetermined distance metrics (such as L1 or L2 norms) offers potential advantages for certain problem domains.

## Major Weaknesses

**1. Incomplete Literature Review and Missing Context**

The Related Work section lacks engagement with several significant prior contributions [1, 2]. The paper would benefit from more thorough positioning relative to existing work. Notably, SA-PINNs [1] similarly involves learning task-specific loss functions, yet this connection is not adequately discussed or distinguished from the proposed approach.

**2. Insufficient Theoretical Justification**

The authors argue that L2 loss lacks theoretical grounding but fail to provide clear explanation of the specific scenarios and mechanisms by which L2 loss becomes problematic. More critically, DEQGAN itself lacks theoretical justification for the learned loss function. The paper does not establish why the discriminator-learned objective should be superior to standard formulations.

**3. Questionable Conceptual Foundation**

The loss function design is adapted from the GAN framework, where the original formulation ensures that the generator minimizes Jensen-Shannon divergence between generated and target distributions under optimal discriminator conditions. However, this theoretical motivation does not transfer to the differential equation setting, where a unique solution exists rather than a distribution to match. Even with Gaussian instance noise, minimizing divergence between two Gaussians reduces to standard L2 minimization. The necessity of employing a generative model for this deterministic problem remains unclear.

**4. Experimental Setup Concerns**

The experiments employ artificial differential equations with single, fixed initial conditions per equation. This raises significant concerns about potential selection bias—the authors may have strategically chosen initial conditions that favor DEQGAN's performance, thereby biasing the empirical evaluation.

**5. Training Instability and Reproducibility**

The method exhibits notable training instability, requiring multiple repeated runs per equation with subsequent filtering of poor-performing instances. This practice undermines reproducibility and raises questions about fair performance comparison with baseline methods.

## Minor Weaknesses and Questions

1. The motivation for learning a loss function rests on claims that standard L1/L2 losses lack justification, yet the paper does not explain when or why these losses fail in practice. If L2 loss is genuinely problematic, what concrete evidence demonstrates that the learned loss function addresses these limitations? Can the authors provide theoretical or empirical justification?

2. Computational efficiency comparisons are absent. How does DEQGAN's runtime scale compared to PINNs and recent variants such as SA-PINNs? A complexity analysis would strengthen the practical applicability claims.

3. The evaluation uses only single, deterministic initial conditions. How would DEQGAN generalize to initial conditions sampled from random function spaces?

## Critical Limitations

- The learned loss function lacks theoretical justification, and the mechanism by which the discriminator learns a superior objective remains unexplained.
- Training instability necessitates multiple runs with post-hoc filtering, compromising reproducibility and fair comparison.
- Experimental evaluation relies on artificial problems with carefully selected singular initial conditions, introducing potential selection bias.

## Recommendation

**Rating: 3 – Reject**

The paper presents significant technical concerns, including weak theoretical foundations, inadequate experimental design, and reproducibility issues. While the empirical results appear promising, they are undermined by potential selection bias in the experimental setup and the method's training instability. The conceptual motivation for applying GANs to deterministic differential equation solving is not convincingly established. These issues must be substantially addressed before the work is suitable for publication.

title: ISAAC Newton: Input-based Approximate Curvature for Newton's Method

reviews.summary: The paper addresses the problem of using second-order information in large-scale machine learning tasks (e.g. training neural networks). It proposes a Kronecker-factored Approximate Curvature (KFAC)-approximated, Tikhonov regularized Gauss-Newton (GN) update step. Instead of a single regularization parameter ($\lambda$), the update step in the paper uses two independent regularization parameters ($\lambda_g$ and $\lambda_a$). The paper relates different settings of $\lambda_a$ and $\lambda_g$ to other algorithms (i.e. classical GN and gradient descent). It shows that allowing $\lambda_g$ grows to infinity gives a computationally efficient update step. It provides empirical results on their proposed method.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The update step $\xi^*$ is interesting and practical. The paper provides experiments to show that using $\xi^*$ can lead to lower computation time without sacrificing performance. Experiments are thorough and tested on some fairly large neural network architectures.
2. The update step $\xi$ is a nice, compact way to describe an algorithm for training neural networks. The analysis of $\xi$ provides various ways of interpreting the algorithm in terms of different regularization parameter settings. The properties of $\xi$ are interesting. The analysis is relatively thorough (e.g. showing that any combination of $\lambda_g$ and $\lambda_a$ will still result in a descent direction).
3. Paper is well-written and clear. 

Weaknesses
1. Some of the experiments are not benchmarked to SOTA (which is acknowledged by the paper). This might affect people's willingness to adopt the method in practice.
reviews.questions:
1. Line 70 refers to $H$ in Equation 6. Typo?
2. Is there an intuitive interpretation of the preconditioner $M$ in Remark 2? Does this preconditioning help in terms of reducing the number of iterations needed to reach a solution?
reviews.limitations: This work does not appear to me to have negative social impact.
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

Summary:
The paper addresses the problem of using second-order information in large-scale machine learning tasks (e.g. training neural networks). It proposes a Kronecker-factored Approximate Curvature (KFAC)-approximated, Tikhonov regularized Gauss-Newton (GN) update step. Instead of a single regularization parameter ($\lambda$), the update step in the paper uses two independent regularization parameters ($\lambda_g$ and $\lambda_a$). The paper relates different settings of $\lambda_a$ and $\lambda_g$ to other algorithms (i.e. classical GN and gradient descent). It shows that allowing $\lambda_g$ grows to infinity gives a computationally efficient update step. It provides empirical results on their proposed method.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The update step $\xi^*$ is interesting and practical. The paper provides experiments to show that using $\xi^*$ can lead to lower computation time without sacrificing performance. Experiments are thorough and tested on some fairly large neural network architectures.
2. The update step $\xi$ is a nice, compact way to describe an algorithm for training neural networks. The analysis of $\xi$ provides various ways of interpreting the algorithm in terms of different regularization parameter settings. The properties of $\xi$ are interesting. The analysis is relatively thorough (e.g. showing that any combination of $\lambda_g$ and $\lambda_a$ will still result in a descent direction).
3. Paper is well-written and clear. 

Weaknesses
1. Some of the experiments are not benchmarked to SOTA (which is acknowledged by the paper). This might affect people's willingness to adopt the method in practice.

Questions:
1. Line 70 refers to $H$ in Equation 6. Typo?
2. Is there an intuitive interpretation of the preconditioner $M$ in Remark 2? Does this preconditioning help in terms of reducing the number of iterations needed to reach a solution?

Limitations:
This work does not appear to me to have negative social impact.

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Summary

This manuscript tackles the challenge of incorporating curvature information into large-scale machine learning optimization, particularly for neural network training. The authors propose a novel optimization update based on a Tikhonov-regularized Gauss-Newton method with KFAC approximation. A key innovation is the introduction of two distinct regularization parameters ($\lambda_g$ and $\lambda_a$) rather than a single regularizer. The paper establishes connections between various parameter configurations and existing optimization algorithms (standard GN and vanilla gradient descent), demonstrates that allowing $\lambda_g$ to approach infinity yields a computationally tractable update, and validates the approach through empirical evaluation.

## Strengths

1. The proposed update step $\xi^*$ presents both theoretical interest and practical utility. The experimental validation convincingly demonstrates that $\xi^*$ achieves computational efficiency gains while maintaining or improving solution quality. The evaluation encompasses reasonably large-scale neural network models and appears comprehensive.

2. The formulation of $\xi$ offers an elegant and unified framework for understanding neural network optimization. Through systematic analysis of $\xi$, the authors provide multiple interpretations of their algorithm through the lens of different regularization parameter choices. The theoretical properties are well-explored, including rigorous verification that all valid parameter combinations preserve descent properties. The analytical treatment is sufficiently detailed and rigorous.

3. The manuscript is clearly articulated and accessible. The presentation effectively communicates both the technical content and intuition.

## Weaknesses

1. Several experimental comparisons do not include evaluation against state-of-the-art baselines, a limitation the authors acknowledge. This gap may reduce practical adoption prospects and confidence in the method's competitiveness.

## Questions and Clarifications Needed

1. There appears to be a potential typographical error on line 70, where $H$ is referenced in connection with Equation 6. Could this be verified?

2. Can the authors provide intuitive insight into the preconditioner $M$ discussed in Remark 2? Additionally, does this preconditioning strategy demonstrably reduce the iteration count required for convergence?

## Limitations and Ethical Considerations

The work presents no apparent negative societal implications or ethical concerns requiring further discussion.

## Overall Assessment and Recommendation

**Rating: 7 - Accept**

This is a technically sound contribution with substantial impact potential in its area of focus. The work demonstrates moderate-to-high significance across multiple dimensions, supported by thorough evaluation, adequate experimental rigor, and reproducibility. No significant ethical or methodological issues remain unaddressed. The paper merits publication and will be of interest to the optimization and deep learning communities.

1. Line 70 refers to $H$ in Equation 6. Typo?
2. Is there an intuitive interpretation of the preconditioner $M$ in Remark 2? Does this preconditioning help in terms of reducing the number of iterations needed to reach a solution?

Limitations:
This work does not appear to me to have negative social impact.

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

reviews.summary:
This paper proposed a KFAC variant to improve the computation efficiency. It introduces two Tikhonov regularization terms into KFAC, and makes gradient conditioning more efficient by force $\lambda_g$ go to infinity. The authors present a theoretical guarantee that the optimization still goes in the right direction even with such an adaptation. The authors also show ISAAC is more computation efficient in small-batch stochastic regimes.

However, I feel contributions in this paper are rather limited, and some motivations might need further justification.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strength:

The paper is well written. Preliminaries and methods are clearly explained. 

Weaknesses:

1, Contributions are limited. Reducing the compute costs of KFAC is a good problem. However, I am not convinced that separating the regularization term and using a large regularization is a novel and effective solution. It only makes the optimizer behave more and more like SGD. As the authors stated in Theorem 1, small $\lambda_a, \lambda_g$ leads to KFAC, while large $\lambda_a, \lambda_g$ leads to vanilla gradient descent. 

2, Empirical results need to be enhanced. The authors use several empirical results to support the claim that ISAAC still behaves like KFAC even though using a strong regularization factor. However, I do not think current results are strong enough to support the claim. The issues in the empirical evaluations also make me doubt the contributions in the paper. 

- 2.1, The author should provide more baselines (Adam, SGD with momentum) so that we can really have a fair evaluation. 

- 2.2, For Figure 1, my understanding is that the authors are trying to show ISAAC still obtains good results (loss and accuracy) even with large regularization. If so, I do not think it is necessary. As the authors stated in Theorem 1, larger regularization makes ISAAC behave more like SGD. Therefore, getting similar loss and accuracy is no surprise.
    
- 2.3, For Figure 2, there are several issues. First, hyper parameters are not fully revealed. For instance, what is the weight decay for each optimizer since weight decays large affects training convergence?  Second, only the training curve is reported. The author really should present both a training and validation curve. In many cases, we can have faster convergence on training dataset, but not for validation dataset. 

- 2.4, For Figure 6, the reported accuracy is obviously lower compared to results we usually have on CIFAR-10. The reason might be that the authors use SGD without momentum. However, I am not convinced under such a setting. SGD with momentum is a default setting in many training tasks. Without momentum, I do not think the results will provide meaningful comparisons and conclusions.

3, Training with small batches should be further justified. I understand the authors stress the compute benefits of ISAAC in a small-batch regime (Theorem 3-ii). However, considering more general settings, the authors should well justify it: is it worth accelerating the training using small batches given the fact that large batches can also improve convergence performance even using SGD. Therefore, I think not only should the author provide results in small-batch regimes, training using large batches should also be evaluated and compared with other baselines.
reviews.questions: See my comments above.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review

## Summary

This work proposes ISAAC, a computationally efficient variant of KFAC that incorporates two Tikhonov regularization terms to reduce computational overhead. The key innovation involves allowing the gradient conditioning regularization parameter $\lambda_g$ to approach infinity, thereby streamlining the computation while maintaining directional consistency in optimization (as established in Theorem 1). The authors demonstrate through theoretical analysis and empirical evaluation that ISAAC achieves competitive performance compared to KFAC, particularly in small-batch training scenarios.

Despite these contributions, the novelty appears incremental, and the empirical validation requires substantial strengthening.

## Strengths

The manuscript is clearly written with well-organized preliminaries and methodology sections that facilitate understanding of the proposed approach.

## Weaknesses

**1. Limited novelty of the core contribution.** While addressing computational efficiency in KFAC is a worthwhile objective, the proposed solution—decoupling regularization terms and employing aggressive regularization—lacks sufficient novelty and convincing justification. The approach essentially drives the optimizer toward vanilla stochastic gradient descent as regularization parameters increase, which is acknowledged in Theorem 1 (small $\lambda_a, \lambda_g$ recovers KFAC; large values approach standard gradient descent). This fundamental trade-off raises questions about whether the contribution represents a meaningful advance or merely a straightforward interpolation between existing methods.

**2. Empirical evaluation requires substantial improvements.** The current experimental results inadequately support the central claims. Several specific concerns arise:

- **2.1 Missing standard baselines.** The evaluation lacks comparisons against widely-used optimizers such as Adam and SGD with momentum, which would provide essential context for assessing ISAAC's practical utility relative to common alternatives.

- **2.2 Figure 1 interpretation.** The figure appears designed to demonstrate that ISAAC maintains reasonable loss and accuracy despite large regularization parameters. However, this finding is unsurprising given Theorem 1's characterization: if increased regularization naturally pushes the optimizer toward SGD, then obtaining SGD-like performance is an expected outcome rather than a meaningful validation of the method.

- **2.3 Figure 2 limitations.** Critical experimental details are omitted. Specifically, weight decay settings for each optimizer are not disclosed, despite their substantial impact on convergence behavior. Additionally, only training curves are presented; validation curves are essential for assessing generalization, as training-set convergence often diverges from test-set performance.

- **2.4 Figure 6 concerns.** The reported CIFAR-10 accuracies fall notably below standard benchmarks. While the authors employ SGD without momentum, this choice appears problematic. SGD with momentum represents the standard practice in most contemporary training pipelines, and comparisons under non-standard settings (momentum-free SGD) limit the practical relevance and interpretability of the results.

**3. Insufficient justification for small-batch training focus.** While Theorem 3-ii establishes computational advantages in small-batch regimes, the practical motivation for this setting deserves deeper examination. Large-batch training offers independent convergence benefits even with standard SGD; therefore, the paper should substantiate why accelerating small-batch training constitutes a sufficiently important objective. The evaluation should encompass both small-batch and large-batch scenarios with comprehensive comparisons to relevant baselines, rather than focusing exclusively on small-batch settings.

## Questions

See detailed comments above.

## Limitations

None identified.

## Recommendation

**Rating: 5 (Borderline accept)** — The paper presents technically sound work with adequate theoretical grounding; however, the contribution is somewhat incremental and the experimental validation, while showing promise, falls short of the standard expected for strong acceptance. The reasons supporting acceptance marginally outweigh those favoring rejection, though the empirical evaluation would benefit from substantial revision prior to publication.

reviews.summary:
In unconstrained optimisation, Newton's method uses inverse Hessian to determine the step size of the update. When applied to deep learning, the sheer size of the Hessian matrix makes Newton's method intractable. Among existing attempts to address this challenge, K-FAC is a promising approach. In practice, Tikhonov regularization/damping is used to avoid divergence.

In this paper, the authors introduced an extension to the standard Tikhonov damping for K-FAC. The main idea is to use two independent damping parameters (hyper-parameters for regularisation) instead of one damping parameter, one for the gradient factor and one for the activity factor, as shown in Equation (17). The authors explored special cases where the two parameters are either 0 or infinity. They also discussed the special case of setting one parameter to infinity, effectively removing the gradient factor in K-FAC. One major claim was that this activity-only form has an advantage in reduced computational complexity, although I don't find a theoretical comparison with K-FAC in this aspect. 

The claim for computational efficiency was mostly supported via empirical evaluation.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

1. The paper addresses an important problem: efficient approximation of Gaussian-Newton approximation
2. The exposition is mostly clear, especially if you are already familiar with the existing literature.
3. The experiments seem comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

1. It is not clear what is the main contribution of this paper. Is it the two-parameter damping mechanism (What advantage do we gain)? Or is it the special case mentioned in Remark 3 (Why this is a still good approximation without $g^{\top}g$)? 
2. Many theoretical results presented look rather trivial.
reviews.questions: What is the intuition behind Eq (21)? Compared with Eq (13), why is it a good approximation?

Summary:
In unconstrained optimisation, Newton's method uses inverse Hessian to determine the step size of the update. When applied to deep learning, the sheer size of the Hessian matrix makes Newton's method intractable. Among existing attempts to address this challenge, K-FAC is a promising approach. In practice, Tikhonov regularization/damping is used to avoid divergence.

In this paper, the authors introduced an extension to the standard Tikhonov damping for K-FAC. The main idea is to use two independent damping parameters (hyper-parameters for regularisation) instead of one damping parameter, one for the gradient factor and one for the activity factor, as shown in Equation (17). The authors explored special cases where the two parameters are either 0 or infinity. They also discussed the special case of setting one parameter to infinity, effectively removing the gradient factor in K-FAC. One major claim was that this activity-only form has an advantage in reduced computational complexity, although I don't find a theoretical comparison with K-FAC in this aspect. 

The claim for computational efficiency was mostly supported via empirical evaluation.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:

1. The paper addresses an important problem: efficient approximation of Gaussian-Newton approximation
2. The exposition is mostly clear, especially if you are already familiar with the existing literature.
3. The experiments seem comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

1. It is not clear what is the main contribution of this paper. Is it the two-parameter damping mechanism (What advantage do we gain)? Or is it the special case mentioned in Remark 3 (Why this is a still good approximation without $g^{\top}g$)? 
2. Many theoretical results presented look rather trivial.

Questions:
What is the intuition behind Eq (21)? Compared with Eq (13), why is it a good approximation?

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

Newton's method in unconstrained optimization leverages the inverse Hessian to guide step sizes during updates. However, applying this approach to deep learning becomes computationally prohibitive due to the dimensionality of the Hessian matrix. K-FAC represents a notable effort to overcome this limitation, though practitioners typically employ Tikhonov regularization or damping strategies to ensure numerical stability during training.

This work proposes a generalization of standard Tikhonov damping within the K-FAC framework by introducing a pair of distinct damping hyperparameters—one governing the gradient component and one governing the activation component, as formalized in Equation (17). The authors investigate boundary conditions where these parameters take extreme values (zero or infinity) and examine a particularly interesting case where the gradient component is removed entirely via infinite damping (detailed in Remark 3). A central assertion is that this activity-only variant yields computational savings relative to standard K-FAC; however, this claim lacks rigorous theoretical substantiation through formal complexity analysis.

The computational efficiency argument is primarily validated through experimental results rather than theoretical derivation.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

**Strengths:**

1. The paper tackles a significant challenge: developing efficient approximations to the Gaussian-Newton matrix for deep learning
2. The presentation is generally accessible, particularly for readers with background knowledge of related methods
3. The experimental evaluation appears sufficiently thorough and well-designed

**Weaknesses:**

1. The primary contribution remains ambiguous. Does the value proposition lie in the dual-parameter damping mechanism itself (and if so, what specific benefits does it confer)? Or does it center on the special case discussed in Remark 3 (and what justifies treating this as a reliable approximation despite omitting the $g^{\top}g$ term)? This distinction needs clarification.
2. Several theoretical findings presented throughout the manuscript appear straightforward or lack sufficient depth to constitute meaningful contributions

## Questions for Authors

Could the authors provide intuition explaining Equation (21)? In particular, relative to Equation (13), what properties or arguments establish that this represents a sound approximation?

## Recommendation

**5: Borderline accept** – This paper demonstrates technical competence and presents a coherent contribution, with merits that marginally outweigh limitations. The work would benefit from stronger theoretical justification, yet the empirical validation and practical relevance provide sufficient grounds for acceptance, though with reservations about the scope and novelty of the core ideas.

When applied to deep learning, the sheer size of the Hessian matrix makes Newton's method intractable. Among existing attempts to address this challenge, K-FAC is a promising approach. Weaknesses:

reviews.summary: Inspired by the KFAC approximation and the Tikhonov regularization of the Newton method, this paper proposes a regularized K-FAC approximated Gauss-Newton type method called ISAAC. The proposed method is based on only the input to a respective layer, therefore does not suffer from a substantial computational overhead. Numerical experiments show the advantages of the proposed method compared to both first-order type and second-order type methods.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

Regularization parameters is crucial for second-order type methods.  The authors first approximate the GGN matrix as a kroncker product of two matrices, and then view two regulation parameters independently. By letting the two regularization parameters vanish or go to infinity, one can obtain new directions and therefore new methods. The proposed method is more efficient than KFAC since only one matrix should be inverted and no need extra propagation to compute $g$.  

Weaknesses:

This paper should be polished to make it easier to read. 

Some related literatures on second-order methods should be mentioned, such as Shampoo[1], KBFGS[2], NG+[3] and SENG[4]. 

Some theoretical properties is natural and trivial. It is better to formalize an algorithm and give a convergence result.

It is better to add some large numerical examples, such as Resnet50 with Imagenet-1k and make a comparison with other second-order methods Shampoo[1], KBFGS[2], NG+[3] and SENG[4]. 

[1] Gupta,V.,Koren,T., Singer,Y.:, Shampoo: Preconditioned stochastic tensor optimization. In: International Conference on Machine Learning.

[2] Goldfarb, D., Ren, Y., Bahamou, A.:, Practical quasi-Newton methods for training deep neural networks. In:Advances inNeural Information Processing Systems.

[3] Yang, M., Xu, D., Cui, Q., Wen, Z., & Xu, P. (2021). NG+: A Multi-Step Matrix-Product Natural Gradient Method for Deep Learning. arXiv preprint arXiv:2106.07454.

[4] Yang, M., Xu, D., Wen, Z., Chen, M., & Xu, P. (2020). Sketch-based empirical natural gradient methods for deep learning. Journal of Scientific Computing.

It is better to add the discussion between GGN matrix and Fisher matrix, which can refer to [1,2] and related literatures.

It is better to add a left brackets after $\eta$ and a right bracket before the gradient in eq.(16). Please also add brackets in a suitable place in eq. (17).

In eq.(19), the gradient should be $\mathrm{g}^\top a /b$ where the $b$ is missed. In eq.(19), the authors use the same batch to approximate the GGN matrix and compute the gradient. Can the batches for approximation of the GGN and the computation of the gradient be different? In that case, what the related theoretical properties will be?

When the objective function is convex, the GGN matrix is positive semi-definite matrix. Adding a regularization parameter in GGN, It is t easy to show that $\zeta$ is an ascent direction, which means Theorem 2 is trivial.

The authors consider the fully-connected neural network in this paper. I believe this method can be extended to convolutional neural networks. And in numerical part, the authors have done some experiments. I wonder can we obtain some explanation from theory for this case? 

For the kronecker approximation of the GGN matrix, $\mathrm{a}\mathrm{a}^\top$ is more important than $\overline{g}^\top\overline{g}$ part. I once validate it numerically. One reason is that each value of the matrix $\overline{g}^\top\overline{g}$  is very small so that the $\lambda_g$ domains. I have noticed one related work recently. The work is named NG+[3].

[1] Botev, Aleksandar, Hippolyt Ritter, and David Barber. "Practical gauss-newton optimisation for deep learning." International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2017.

[2] Martens, James. "New insights and perspectives on the natural gradient method." The Journal of Machine Learning Research 21.1 (2020): 5776-5851.

[3] Yang, M., Xu, D., Cui, Q., Wen, Z., & Xu, P. (2021). NG+: A Multi-Step Matrix-Product Natural Gradient Method for Deep Learning. arXiv preprint arXiv:2106.07454.

Summary:
Inspired by the KFAC approximation and the Tikhonov regularization of the Newton method, this paper proposes a regularized K-FAC approximated Gauss-Newton type method called ISAAC. The proposed method is based on only the input to a respective layer, therefore does not suffer from a substantial computational overhead. Numerical experiments show the advantages of the proposed method compared to both first-order type and second-order type methods.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:

Regularization parameters is crucial for second-order type methods.  The authors first approximate the GGN matrix as a kroncker product of two matrices, and then view two regulation parameters independently. By letting the two regularization parameters vanish or go to infinity, one can obtain new directions and therefore new methods. The proposed method is more efficient than KFAC since only one matrix should be inverted and no need extra propagation to compute $g$.  

Weaknesses:

This paper should be polished to make it easier to read. 

Some related literatures on second-order methods should be mentioned, such as Shampoo[1], KBFGS[2], NG+[3] and SENG[4]. 

Some theoretical properties is natural and trivial. It is better to formalize an algorithm and give a convergence result.

It is better to add some large numerical examples, such as Resnet50 with Imagenet-1k and make a comparison with other second-order methods Shampoo[1], KBFGS[2], NG+[3] and SENG[4].

[4] Yang, M., Xu, D., Wen, Z., Chen, M., & Xu, P. (2020). Sketch-based empirical natural gradient methods for deep learning. Journal of Scientific Computing.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten Version

## Summary

Drawing inspiration from both the KFAC framework and Tikhonov-regularized Newton approaches, the authors introduce ISAAC, a regularized K-FAC-based Gauss-Newton method. A key advantage of this approach is its reliance solely on layer-specific inputs, which substantially reduces computational demands. The empirical evaluation demonstrates competitive performance relative to both conventional first-order optimizers and existing second-order techniques.

## Strengths

The paper makes a valuable contribution by recognizing that regularization parameters are fundamental to the effective application of second-order methods. The authors' strategy of decomposing the GGN matrix as a Kronecker product and subsequently treating the two regularization parameters as independent entities is insightful. By allowing these parameters to approach limiting values, the method generates alternative search directions and yields distinct algorithmic variants. Compared to KFAC, the proposed approach exhibits improved computational efficiency since it requires inversion of only a single matrix and avoids the additional forward/backward propagation overhead needed to compute g.

## Weaknesses

**Presentation and Scope**: The manuscript would benefit from additional polishing to enhance clarity and readability. The related work section is incomplete; important references on second-order optimization methods including Shampoo, KBFGS, NG+, and SENG should be incorporated and discussed.

**Theoretical Analysis**: Several theoretical properties presented in the paper appear straightforward and lack substantive novelty. The work would be strengthened by formalizing a complete algorithm and providing rigorous convergence guarantees.

**Experimental Evaluation**: The numerical experiments are limited in scope. The paper should include larger-scale benchmarks such as ResNet50 trained on ImageNet-1k, along with systematic comparisons against competing second-order methods (Shampoo, KBFGS, NG+, SENG).

**Technical Issues**:
- Line 60: References should be consolidated as [14,15] rather than [14],[15]; please audit the entire document for consistency.
- The relationship between the GGN matrix and Fisher information matrix deserves explicit discussion, with appropriate citations.
- Equation (16) requires additional bracketing around η and the gradient term; equation (17) similarly needs clarification through proper bracket placement.
- In equation (19), the gradient expression should read g^T a/b with the denominator b explicitly included. Additionally, the authors employ the same batch for both GGN matrix approximation and gradient computation—can these use different batches? What are the implications for the theoretical guarantees in this scenario?
- Theorem 2 appears trivial in the convex setting: when the objective is convex, the GGN matrix is positive semi-definite, and adding regularization straightforwardly ensures ζ is an ascent direction.

**Scope of Analysis**: While the paper focuses on fully-connected networks, the authors demonstrate experiments on convolutional architectures. Providing theoretical justification for why the method extends successfully to CNNs would strengthen the contribution.

**Kronecker Approximation**: The component aa^T dominates over ḡ^T ḡ in the Kronecker factorization—this has been numerically validated in prior work. One contributing factor is that entries of ḡ^T ḡ are typically small, allowing λ_g to become dominant. The recent work NG+ addresses related concerns and should be discussed.

## Questions and Minor Issues

- Reference formatting needs standardization throughout.
- The discussion of GGN versus Fisher information should be expanded with proper citations.
- Bracket notation in equations (16) and (17) requires revision for clarity.
- The treatment of different batches for GGN approximation versus gradient computation needs clarification.

## Overall Assessment

This paper presents a technically sound contribution to second-order optimization for deep learning. The core ideas are solid and the method demonstrates practical advantages. However, the work is held back by incomplete literature coverage, limited experimental scope, and theoretical results that lack sufficient depth. With revisions addressing the experimental evaluation, theoretical rigor, and presentation quality, this manuscript would constitute a stronger contribution.

---

**Decision: 5 - Borderline accept**

The paper is technically competent with merit sufficient to warrant publication, though the limited scope of evaluation and presentation issues prevent it from being a strong accept. The reasons favoring acceptance marginally outweigh those for rejection.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
Inspired by the KFAC approximation and the Tikhonov regularization of the Newton method, this paper proposes a regularized K-FAC approximated Gauss-Newton type method called ISAAC. The proposed method is based on only the input to a respective layer, therefore does not suffer from a substantial computational overhead. Numerical experiments show the advantages of the proposed method compared to both first-order type and second-order type methods.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:

Regularization parameters is crucial for second-order type methods.  The authors first approximate the GGN matrix as a kroncker product of two matrices, and then view two regulation parameters independently. By letting the two regularization parameters vanish or go to infinity, one can obtain new directions and therefore new methods. The proposed method is more efficient than KFAC since only one matrix should be inverted and no need extra propagation to compute $g$.  

Weaknesses:

This paper should be polished to make it easier to read. 

Some related literatures on second-order methods should be mentioned, such as Shampoo[1], KBFGS[2], NG+[3] and SENG[4]. 

Some theoretical properties is natural and trivial. It is better to formalize an algorithm and give a convergence result.

It is better to add some large numerical examples, such as Resnet50 with Imagenet-1k and make a comparison with other second-order methods Shampoo[1], KBFGS[2], NG+[3] and SENG[4]. 

[1] Gupta,V.,Koren,T., Singer,Y.:, Shampoo: Preconditioned stochastic tensor optimization. In: International Conference on Machine Learning.

[2] Goldfarb, D., Ren, Y., Bahamou, A.:, Practical quasi-Newton methods for training deep neural networks. In:Advances inNeural Information Processing Systems.

[3] Yang, M., Xu, D., Cui, Q., Wen, Z., & Xu, P. (2021). NG+: A Multi-Step Matrix-Product Natural Gradient Method for Deep Learning. arXiv preprint arXiv:2106.07454.

[4] Yang, M., Xu, D., Wen, Z., Chen, M., & Xu, P. (2020). Sketch-based empirical natural gradient methods for deep learning. Journal of Scientific Computing.

Questions:
line 60: change [14],[15] to [14.15]. It is better to put multiple references in the same brackets. Please check other places.

It is better to add the discussion between GGN matrix and Fisher matrix, which can refer to [1,2] and related literatures.

It is better to add a left brackets after $\eta$ and a right bracket before the gradient in eq.(16). Please also add brackets in a suitable place in eq. (17).

In eq.(19), the gradient should be $\mathrm{g}^\top a /b$ where the $b$ is missed. In eq.(19), the authors use the same batch to approximate the GGN matrix and compute the gradient. Can the batches for approximation of the GGN and the computation of the gradient be different? In that case, what the related theoretical properties will be?

When the objective function is convex, the GGN matrix is positive semi-definite matrix. Adding a regularization parameter in GGN, It is t easy to show that $\zeta$ is an ascent direction, which means Theorem 2 is trivial.

The authors consider the fully-connected neural network in this paper. I believe this method can be extended to convolutional neural networks. And in numerical part, the authors have done some experiments. I wonder can we obtain some explanation from theory for this case? 

For the kronecker approximation of the GGN matrix, $\mathrm{a}\mathrm{a}^\top$ is more important than $\overline{g}^\top\overline{g}$ part. I once validate it numerically. One reason is that each value of the matrix $\overline{g}^\top\overline{g}$  is very small so that the $\lambda_g$ domains. I have noticed one related work recently. The work is named NG+[3].

[1] Botev, Aleksandar, Hippolyt Ritter, and David Barber. "Practical gauss-newton optimisation for deep learning." International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2017.

[2] Martens, James. "New insights and perspectives on the natural gradient method." The Journal of Machine Learning Research 21.1 (2020): 5776-5851.

[3] Yang, M., Xu, D., Cui, Q., Wen, Z., & Xu, P. (2021). NG+: A Multi-Step Matrix-Product Natural Gradient Method for Deep Learning. arXiv preprint arXiv:2106.07454.

Limitations:
Yes

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
